texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: NON-PARAMETRIC ONLINE MARKET REGIME DETECTION AND REGIME CLUSTERING FOR MULTIDIMENSIONAL AND PATH-DEPENDENT DATA STRUCTURES
texts.text: NON-PARAMETRIC ONLINE MARKET REGIME DETECTION AND REGIME CLUSTERING FOR MULTIDIMENSIONAL AND PATH-DEPENDENT DATA STRUCTURES

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: BLANKA HORVATH 1 , 2 , 3 AND ZACHARIA ISSA ∗ 4
texts.text: BLANKA HORVATH 1 , 2 , 3 AND ZACHARIA ISSA ∗ 4

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Abstract. In this work we present a non-parametric online market regime detection method for multidimensional data structures using a path-wise two-sample test derived from a maximum mean discrepancy-based similarity metric on path space that uses rough path signatures as a feature map. The latter similarity metric has been developed and applied as a discriminator in recent generative models for small data environments, and has been optimised here to the setting where the size of new incoming data is particularly small, for faster reactivity. On the same principles, we also present a path-wise method for regime clustering which extends our previous work [HIM21]. The presented regime clustering techniques, as in [HIM21], were designed as ex-ante market analysis tools that can identify periods of approximatively similar market activity, but the new results also apply to path-wise, high dimensional-, and to non-Markovian settings as well as to data structures that exhibit autocorrelation. We demonstrate our clustering tools on easily verifiable synthetic datasets of increasing complexity, and also show how the outlined regime detection techniques can be used as fast on-line automatic regime change detectors or as outlier detection tools, including a fully automated pipeline. Finally, we apply the fine-tuned algorithms to real-world historical data including high-dimensional baskets of equities and the recent price evolution of crypto assets, and we show that our methodology swiftly and accurately indicated historical periods of market turmoil.
texts.text: Abstract. In this work we present a non-parametric online market regime detection method for multidimensional data structures using a path-wise two-sample test derived from a maximum mean discrepancy-based similarity metric on path space that uses rough path signatures as a feature map. The latter similarity metric has been developed and applied as a discriminator in recent generative models for small data environments, and has been optimised here to the setting where the size of new incoming data is particularly small, for faster reactivity. On the same principles, we also present a path-wise method for regime clustering which extends our previous work [HIM21]. The presented regime clustering techniques, as in [HIM21], were designed as ex-ante market analysis tools that can identify periods of approximatively similar market activity, but the new results also apply to path-wise, high dimensional-, and to non-Markovian settings as well as to data structures that exhibit autocorrelation. We demonstrate our clustering tools on easily verifiable synthetic datasets of increasing complexity, and also show how the outlined regime detection techniques can be used as fast on-line automatic regime change detectors or as outlier detection tools, including a fully automated pipeline. Finally, we apply the fine-tuned algorithms to real-world historical data including high-dimensional baskets of equities and the recent price evolution of crypto assets, and we show that our methodology swiftly and accurately indicated historical periods of market turmoil.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. Introduction and problem setting
texts.text: 1. Introduction and problem setting

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Time series data derived from asset returns are known to exhibit certain properties, termed stylised facts , that are consistently prevalent across asset classes and markets. To name a few, asset price returns are widely accepted to be non-stationary and to exhibit volatility clustering. We refer the reader to [Con01] for a thorough discussion of such properties. In this article we will turn our attention to one property in particular, the heteroscedastic nature of financial time series, since it is of imminent practical relevance to financial analysts and quants for a multitude of practical applications. In this context, one may be interested in whether a given asset returns series-or a set of series, in case of multiple assets-can be divided into periods in which the (random) asset price dynamics can be attributed to the same (up to a small margin of imprecision) underlying distribution. Such periods are often referred to as market regimes . The task of grouping these regimes the market regime clustering problem (MRCP) . This article is devoted to the online detection of changes in such regimes, i.e. to developing tools that help us recognise in real time (as data comes in) if a shift in the underlying regime is happening. We will refer to this as (online) market regime detection problem (MRDP) .
texts.text: Time series data derived from asset returns are known to exhibit certain properties, termed stylised facts , that are consistently prevalent across asset classes and markets. To name a few, asset price returns are widely accepted to be non-stationary and to exhibit volatility clustering. We refer the reader to [Con01] for a thorough discussion of such properties. In this article we will turn our attention to one property in particular, the heteroscedastic nature of financial time series, since it is of imminent practical relevance to financial analysts and quants for a multitude of practical applications. In this context, one may be interested in whether a given asset returns series-or a set of series, in case of multiple assets-can be divided into periods in which the (random) asset price dynamics can be attributed to the same (up to a small margin of imprecision) underlying distribution. Such periods are often referred to as market regimes . The task of grouping these regimes the market regime clustering problem (MRCP) . This article is devoted to the online detection of changes in such regimes, i.e. to developing tools that help us recognise in real time (as data comes in) if a shift in the underlying regime is happening. We will refer to this as (online) market regime detection problem (MRDP) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 1.1. Relation to existing literature: In classical statistics the problem we address in this work is related to problems studied under the umbrella of change point detection (CPD) methods. Among these, our setting is closest to on-line change point detection, although our algorithm has a somewhat different flavour than many similar methods in the literature. The term refers to the localization of change points , that is to the detection of instances of abrupt distributional changes in ordered observations, such as time series. CPD has been studied over the last several decades in numerous scientific fields including statistics, computer science, and data mining. Applications include a broad range of important real-world problems, from medical conditioning and speech recognition to climate change detection. While each of these problems has its bespoke challenges, our method is inspired by financial applications (such as optimal investment and (deep) hedging and pricing) and is intended as an early (on-line) indicator of distributional changes in financial time series. We have fine tuned our algorithm to such applications, potentially at the expense of features that might be relevant for other fields (e.g. speech recognition). For a strenuously thorough and comprehensive account of associated methods, see the 2016 survey [AC17], and for a more recent review of statistical techniques related to CPD, see [TOV20]. Finally, for some of the most recent related literature we refer the reader to the literature review in [LBK22].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the classical statistics literature, change point detection methods broadly fall in one of the two categories: Parametric, and non-parametric change point methods: (i) Parametric change point methods typically assume that the observations in between change points stem from a finite dimensional family of parametric (often Gaussian or exponential) distributions. (ii) Non-parametric change point detection methods use measures that do not rely on parametric forms of the distribution or the nature of change.
texts.text: In the classical statistics literature, change point detection methods broadly fall in one of the two categories: Parametric, and non-parametric change point methods: (i) Parametric change point methods typically assume that the observations in between change points stem from a finite dimensional family of parametric (often Gaussian or exponential) distributions. (ii) Non-parametric change point detection methods use measures that do not rely on parametric forms of the distribution or the nature of change.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: From a different point of view, change point detection algorithms can be classified as (a) 'offline' or (b) 'online'. (a) Offline algorithms consider the entire data set at once, and look back in time to recognize where the change occurred. In contrast, (b) online (or real-time), algorithms run concurrently with the process they are monitoring, processing each data point as it becomes available, with a goal of detecting a change point as soon as possible after it occurs. In practice, no change point detection algorithm operates in perfect real time because it must inspect new data before determining if a change point occurred between the old and new data points. Different (online) algorithms require different amounts of new data before change point detection can occur. Based on this, [AC17] defines the term ' ϵ -real time algorithm' to refer to an online algorithm which needs at least ϵ data samples in the new batch of data to detect change points. Smaller ϵ values imply more responsive (stronger) change point detection algorithms.
texts.text: From a different point of view, change point detection algorithms can be classified as (a) 'offline' or (b) 'online'. (a) Offline algorithms consider the entire data set at once, and look back in time to recognize where the change occurred. In contrast, (b) online (or real-time), algorithms run concurrently with the process they are monitoring, processing each data point as it becomes available, with a goal of detecting a change point as soon as possible after it occurs. In practice, no change point detection algorithm operates in perfect real time because it must inspect new data before determining if a change point occurred between the old and new data points. Different (online) algorithms require different amounts of new data before change point detection can occur. Based on this, [AC17] defines the term ' ϵ -real time algorithm' to refer to an online algorithm which needs at least ϵ data samples in the new batch of data to detect change points. Smaller ϵ values imply more responsive (stronger) change point detection algorithms.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Multivariate setups of change-point detection algorithms are generally considered to be challenging both in (i) parametric scenarios, but even more so in (ii) non-parametric scenarios, see [LBK22]. There is a limited number of contributions in the multivariate non-parametric setup available. The non-parametric multivariate change point methods we are aware of are based on one of the following approaches: Ranks ([LYFLLC18]), distances ([MJ14, HMN15, ZC21]) and some are based on kernel methods, such as kernel distances [ACH15, GA18], and kernel densities.
texts.text: Multivariate setups of change-point detection algorithms are generally considered to be challenging both in (i) parametric scenarios, but even more so in (ii) non-parametric scenarios, see [LBK22]. There is a limited number of contributions in the multivariate non-parametric setup available. The non-parametric multivariate change point methods we are aware of are based on one of the following approaches: Ranks ([LYFLLC18]), distances ([MJ14, HMN15, ZC21]) and some are based on kernel methods, such as kernel distances [ACH15, GA18], and kernel densities.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Classically, the idea behind these methods is a form of hypothesis testing based on a test statistic that measures the dissimilarity of distributions. The method we develop in this paper is similar-in its core-to this line of thoughts, but with some key differences (which we detail in section 1.2) and with the twist that we make use of path-based methods for this task.
texts.text: Classically, the idea behind these methods is a form of hypothesis testing based on a test statistic that measures the dissimilarity of distributions. The method we develop in this paper is similar-in its core-to this line of thoughts, but with some key differences (which we detail in section 1.2) and with the twist that we make use of path-based methods for this task.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the more recent era of statistics with machine learning (ML), a large range of nonparametric methods are available to learn complex conditional classes of probability distributions (see for example recent works on conditional Market Generators [BHL + 20, IHLS23, WBWB19]). Making use of these ML-based non-parametric methods for change point detection purposes have proven (in direct comparison with the aforementioned classical methods) to often outperform simple rank and distance-based methods: [Fri04] proposed to use binary classifiers for two- sample testing, [HMN22, LBK22] applied this framework in combination with with random forests and [LPO17] with deep neural networks.
texts.text: In the more recent era of statistics with machine learning (ML), a large range of nonparametric methods are available to learn complex conditional classes of probability distributions (see for example recent works on conditional Market Generators [BHL + 20, IHLS23, WBWB19]). Making use of these ML-based non-parametric methods for change point detection purposes have proven (in direct comparison with the aforementioned classical methods) to often outperform simple rank and distance-based methods: [Fri04] proposed to use binary classifiers for two- sample testing, [HMN22, LBK22] applied this framework in combination with with random forests and [LPO17] with deep neural networks.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In fact, once we have delved into the techniques used on the newly emerging field of Market Generation or Market Simulation (two twin-terms that were coined in the works of Kondratyev and Schwarz [KS19] and Bühler et al. [BHL + 20]), it is clear that the very same tool-the one we use there to verify that the simulated distribution is indistinguishable from the historical one-can be used for the opposite task as well: to indicate if two samples are very unlikely to have been drawn from the same distribution, which would indicate a regime change . In [BHL + 20], a pathwise signature-MMD has been used to evaluate the 'quality' of the generative model's synthetic market paths. We observe that in fact with the same reasoning as above, a 'good' and easily computable metric on path space is a central object for a number of key financial applications, including not only market generation (and discrimination) but regime classification and clustering problems, outlier detection and-given sufficient accuracy-even calibration problems as well as an array of further applications.

[BHL + 20]), it is clear that the very same tool-the one we use there to verify that the simulated distribution is indistinguishable from the historical one-can be used for the opposite task as well: to indicate if two samples are very unlikely to have been drawn from the same distribution, which would indicate a regime change . In [BHL + 20], a pathwise signature-MMD has been used to evaluate the 'quality' of the generative model's synthetic market paths. We observe that in fact with the same reasoning as above, a 'good' and easily computable metric on path space is a central object for a number of key financial applications, including not only market generation (and discrimination) but regime classification and clustering problems, outlier detection and-given sufficient accuracy-even calibration problems as well as an array of further applications.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Of course, the above reasoning about different applications which sounds straightforward in theory poses some intricate modelling challenges if the algorithm is tasked with picking up distributional changes in real time via a sliding window of a financial time series. As we will see later on, the challenge becomes even more intricate if the time series is not an output of a synthetic dataset (generated by the same distribution) but rather a real (historical) time series that is inherently heteroscedastic and non-stationary.
texts.text: Of course, the above reasoning about different applications which sounds straightforward in theory poses some intricate modelling challenges if the algorithm is tasked with picking up distributional changes in real time via a sliding window of a financial time series. As we will see later on, the challenge becomes even more intricate if the time series is not an output of a synthetic dataset (generated by the same distribution) but rather a real (historical) time series that is inherently heteroscedastic and non-stationary.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 1.2. Our contribution in this work. A key difference between our work and the non-parametric method by [LBK22] and the contributions presented in the review article [TOV20], lies in our pronounced aim for real-time applicability of the method. While the aforementioned methods focus on offline problems, that is, their aim is to retrospectively detect changes after all samples have been observed, our present work-in contrast-develops an algorithm that can be used as an early indicator of shifts in market regimes in real time (i.e. online), which relies on a satisfactory accuracy of the algorithm for a relatively moderate number of observations. The latter property ensures that our algorithm can be tuned to be more reactive , i.e. mark potential changes in the underlying distribution of the data within a short time frame, that is, as based on a relatively low number of new incoming observations. The "price" to pay for a reactive methodology comes in terms of its accuracy 1 . One of the difficulties mentioned in the literature is that several non-parametric change point methods only detect changes that are deemed "large enough" considering the noise level and available data. This requirement is necessary to prevent numerous false positives, see [ACH15]. However, with view to the applications we have in mind, we can be somewhat more lenient about 'false positives" than the majority of traditional change point detection methods: Since our goal is an automated early indicator of shifts market regimes, it is more important to pick up on potential changeseven if they happen to ease back into the previous state as time passes-rather than to prioritise the elimination of false positives at all costs. At the same time, we should point out that while signal-to-noise concerns are indeed (justifiably) a limiting factor in most other methods, signal strength is particularly good in our framework, which is a strength of (pathwise) signature-kernel methods in general, but it is a particularly optimised feature of our specific model design and kernel choice in this paper. On a slightly different note, we also argue, that for a number of financial applications (deep pricing and hedging or deep trading or portfolio optimization) it is indeed advantageous to focus our regime detection efforts on large changes (rather than small ones) and to equip the algorithms with robustification tools (to account for small levels of model uncertainty) to ensure that they remain valid in the face of small changes in the data- for example changes that are within a noise and small-data-induced threshold.

A key difference between our work and the non-parametric method by [LBK22] and the contributions presented in the review article [TOV20], lies in our pronounced aim for real-time applicability of the method. While the aforementioned methods focus on offline problems, that is, their aim is to retrospectively detect changes after all samples have been observed, our present work-in contrast-develops an algorithm that can be used as an early indicator of shifts in market regimes in real time (i.e. online), which relies on a satisfactory accuracy of the algorithm for a relatively moderate number of observations. The latter property ensures that our algorithm can be tuned to be more reactive , i.e. mark potential changes in the underlying distribution of the data within a short time frame, that is, as based on a relatively low number of new incoming observations. The "price" to pay for a reactive methodology comes in terms of its accuracy 1 . One of the difficulties mentioned in the literature is that several non-parametric change point methods only detect changes that are deemed "large enough" considering the noise level and available data. This requirement is necessary to prevent numerous false positives, see [ACH15]. However, with view to the applications we have in mind, we can be somewhat more lenient about 'false positives" than the majority of traditional change point detection methods: Since our goal is an automated early indicator of shifts market regimes, it is more important to pick up on potential changeseven if they happen to ease back into the previous state as time passes-rather than to prioritise the elimination of false positives at all costs. At the same time, we should point out that while signal-to-noise concerns are indeed (justifiably) a limiting factor in most other methods, signal strength is particularly good in our framework, which is a strength of (pathwise) signature-kernel methods in general, but it is a particularly optimised feature of our specific model design and kernel choice in this paper. On a slightly different note, we also argue, that for a number of financial applications (deep pricing and hedging or deep trading or portfolio optimization) it is indeed advantageous to focus our regime detection efforts on large changes (rather than small ones) and to equip the algorithms with robustification tools (to account for small levels of model uncertainty) to ensure that they remain valid in the face of small changes in the data- for example changes that are within a noise and small-data-induced threshold.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In our previous work [HIM21], we gave an algorithm which attempts to solve the MRCP by implementing a modified version of the classical k -means algorithm on the space of distributions P ( R ) . This was done by representing asset price paths by the empirical measure associated to segments of the asset's corresponding path of log returns. Equipped with a suitable metric, this methodology was able to detect changes in regime from both synthetically generated and real data. However, this approach is somewhat limited in two aspects: first, auto-correlative effects are discarded when conducting inference on distributions of returns, and second, extending to higher-dimensional price paths poses a computational challenge, as discussed in our previous work [HIM21] which discusses techniques presented in [NDŞB19] and [RPDB11] on this matter.
texts.text: In our previous work [HIM21], we gave an algorithm which attempts to solve the MRCP by implementing a modified version of the classical k -means algorithm on the space of distributions P ( R ) . This was done by representing asset price paths by the empirical measure associated to segments of the asset's corresponding path of log returns. Equipped with a suitable metric, this methodology was able to detect changes in regime from both synthetically generated and real data. However, this approach is somewhat limited in two aspects: first, auto-correlative effects are discarded when conducting inference on distributions of returns, and second, extending to higher-dimensional price paths poses a computational challenge, as discussed in our previous work [HIM21] which discusses techniques presented in [NDŞB19] and [RPDB11] on this matter.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this paper, we outline a different approach to market regime detection: We study distributions on path space as opposed to those on R , and use path signatures as features together with a version of the signature maximum mean discrepancy distance (sig-MMD) introduced in [CO18]. Utilising the path signature in our inference approach means we are able to capture time-dependent effects which were previously not possible.
texts.text: In this paper, we outline a different approach to market regime detection: We study distributions on path space as opposed to those on R , and use path signatures as features together with a version of the signature maximum mean discrepancy distance (sig-MMD) introduced in [CO18]. Utilising the path signature in our inference approach means we are able to capture time-dependent effects which were previously not possible.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: With this in mind, we aim to fine-tune and improve our sig-MMD distance as much as possible: as one can uniformly approximate functions on path space by linear functionals acting in signature space (see for instance [LSD + 20], Theorem 2.1) the more signature terms one takes, the more nuanced picture we have about the observed paths. However, with the truncated signature approach, one quickly faces a trade-off between taking more terms in the truncated signature and facing the curse of dimensionality. Instead, in this paper we take a leap towards kernel methods with a kernel trick (cf. [SCF + 21, LSD + 20, SLL + 21]) to study distributions on pathspace and thus overcome the truncation problem associated to the path signature (cf. [SCF + 21]) and to make a number of further improvements to the original sig-MMD algorithm used in [BHL + 20]. Specifically, using a variant of the kernel methods introduced in [SLL + 21] we are also able to extend dimensionality rather easily, and in fact obtain stronger results in higher-dimensional cases without facing too heavy a computational penalty (see section 2.3). In passing, we also fix a problem of the original truncated sig-MMD approaches connected to conditional distributions: As conditional distributions are closely liked with the filtration of the process, one needs a version of the sig-MMD that is fine enough to carry the filtration information of the stochastic evolution of the process in order to generate (and evaluate) conditional distributions. Without such a finer sig-MMD distance, any conditional analysis (and thus any analysis of path-wise temporal effects) is confined to the Markovian case and strictly speaking one is left to good faith when leaving the Markovian regime. As most settings in real life datasets and financial time series are typically non-Markovian by nature, this would a priori be quite a severe limitation.

However, with the truncated signature approach, one quickly faces a trade-off between taking more terms in the truncated signature and facing the curse of dimensionality. Instead, in this paper we take a leap towards kernel methods with a kernel trick (cf. [SCF + 21, LSD + 20, SLL + 21]) to study distributions on pathspace and thus overcome the truncation problem associated to the path signature (cf. [SCF + 21]) and to make a number of further improvements to the original sig-MMD algorithm used in [BHL + 20]. Specifically, using a variant of the kernel methods introduced in [SLL + 21] we are also able to extend dimensionality rather easily, and in fact obtain stronger results in higher-dimensional cases without facing too heavy a computational penalty (see section 2.3). In passing, we also fix a problem of the original truncated sig-MMD approaches connected to conditional distributions: As conditional distributions are closely liked with the filtration of the process, one needs a version of the sig-MMD that is fine enough to carry the filtration information of the stochastic evolution of the process in order to generate (and evaluate) conditional distributions. Without such a finer sig-MMD distance, any conditional analysis (and thus any analysis of path-wise temporal effects) is confined to the Markovian case and strictly speaking one is left to good faith when leaving the Markovian regime. As most settings in real life datasets and financial time series are typically non-Markovian by nature, this would a priori be quite a severe limitation.

texts.orig: To summarize, our techniques are superior to any method using the original truncated sig-MMD for the following reasons:
texts.text: To summarize, our techniques are superior to any method using the original truncated sig-MMD for the following reasons:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (1) Non-truncation : Since one can employ a 'kernel trick' to explicitly arrive at the signature kernel without needing to truncate the signature mapping at any point 2 .
texts.text: Non-truncation : Since one can employ a 'kernel trick' to explicitly arrive at the signature kernel without needing to truncate the signature mapping at any point 2 .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (2) High dimensionality : The computational cost for calculating the signature kernel between collections of paths is linear in the path dimension, making inference on higher dimensional objects possible and computationally feasible.
texts.text: High dimensionality : The computational cost for calculating the signature kernel between collections of paths is linear in the path dimension, making inference on higher dimensional objects possible and computationally feasible.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (3) Filtration information : By choosing the rank 2 maximum mean discrepancy associated to the signature kernel, we can incorporate filtration information into the inference process. Such information is crucial when wishing to ascertain more than just the finite-dimensional distributions between sets of paths.
texts.text: Filtration information : By choosing the rank 2 maximum mean discrepancy associated to the signature kernel, we can incorporate filtration information into the inference process. Such information is crucial when wishing to ascertain more than just the finite-dimensional distributions between sets of paths.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Finally, we also show that our approach is suitable to tackle both the market regime classification problem (MRCP) and the market regime detection problem (MRDP). The MRDPcan be generalised to a one-class (contemporary) classification procedure, and thus can be framed as a method for anomaly detection. We compare our results to current pathwise techniques in the literature and show that our approach is powerful even in low data environments.
texts.text: Finally, we also show that our approach is suitable to tackle both the market regime classification problem (MRCP) and the market regime detection problem (MRDP). The MRDPcan be generalised to a one-class (contemporary) classification procedure, and thus can be framed as a method for anomaly detection. We compare our results to current pathwise techniques in the literature and show that our approach is powerful even in low data environments.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The paper is structured as follows. In the rest of this section, we give an outline of the MRCP and MRDP. In Section 2, we give a brief overview of path signatures (including those of higher rank), streamed data, and the signature maximum mean discrepancy. In Section 3, we outline the objects and methodologies used in our experiments. Section 4 and Section 5 covers the MRDP and MRCP respectively, where paths are generated synthetically. Finally, Section 6 gives real data experiments, including a study of our approach on both a basket of equities and on cryptocurrency data.

texts.orig: Throughout the paper, objects of the type x will be the main sources of data that we perform analysis on. Essentially, x can be thought of as a segment of a (financial) time series observed over the period [0 , T ] with t 0 = 0 and t N = T . This is typically the price evolution of an asset or index in our examples, formally it can be seen as one realisation of a random path observed at discrete time-points t i , i = 1 , . . . , N that originates from some underlying distribution, which may or may not have changed throughout the observation period [0 , T ] with t 0 = 0 and t N = T .

This is typically the price evolution of an asset or index in our examples, formally it can be seen as one realisation of a random path observed at discrete time-points t i , i = 1 , . . . , N that originates from some underlying distribution, which may or may not have changed throughout the observation period [0 , T ] with t 0 = 0 and t N = T .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In order to formalise what we mean by a regime or a change of regime (that may or may not be present in such an object x ), let us first recall the following classical definitions. Recall that a stationary time series on the horizon [0 , T ] is a finite variance process whose statistical properties are all constant over time. This definition assumes in particular that the mean value function µ t := E [ x t ] is constant on t ∈ [0 , T ] and that the auto-covariance function cov( x s , x t ) = E [( x s -µ s )( x t -µ t )] depends on the timestamps s, t only through their time difference, or | t -s | , but not through their position within [0 , T ] . Given an element x of the set of data streams of fixed length N ∈ N , a stream of all possible sub-sequences of length n can be built by moving a sliding window of size n across the data set. A change point then represents a transition in the process that generates the time series data. For more details on the classical setting, see [AC17].

texts.orig: (in [HIM21] represented as a "barycenter") of distributions that are similar enough to one another from the perspective of our application. The applications we primarily have in mind are quantisation, (robust) deep hedging, and risk management considerations. In the context of deep hedging, we would like to make sure that small deviations in the data generating process used for training does not lead to large errors in the algorithm or the hedging performance, and to this end, we introduce a robustification to the hedging objective. At the same time, if deviations from our reference model become significant and persist over time, then a retraining of the hedging engine is called for. With the tools presented here, we provide a tool-set to distinguish between these two cases.

At the same time, if deviations from our reference model become significant and persist over time, then a retraining of the hedging engine is called for. With the tools presented here, we provide a tool-set to distinguish between these two cases.

texts.orig: Definition 1.3 (Regime) . Let x ∈ S ( R d ) be a stream over R d . Let X i denote the subsequence associated to a windowing operation of size n ∈ N , n ≪ N across x , i.e. X i := ( x i , x i +1 , . . . , x i + n ) for some i on the observation horizon i = 1 , . . . , N . We denote by P X i the underlying probability density of observations X i associated to the window i . A regime (starting at i ) is then the longest consecutive run of indices k , for which it holds that 3 P X i = P X i +1 = · · · = P X i + k . Significant and lasting changes will be referred to as instances of regime change P X i = · · · = P X i + k ∗ ≪ 4 P X i + k ∗ +1 = · · · = P X i + k ∗ + l , with k, k ∗ , l ≫ 0 . Changes of (very) large magnitude but very short duration will be referred to as spikes or outliers , and we will permit ourselves the discretion for changes of sufficiently small magnitude (estimation errors, or changes within a predifined tolerance 5 ) to be disregarded.

texts.orig: Viewing regimes in terms of X means that they are represented as P X i ∈ P ( E ) , where P ( E ) denotes the space of Borel probability measures over E . This was the approach taken in [HIM21] for the case E = R . As mentioned in Section 1 this approach necessarily discards temporal effects due to aggregation; moreover, it does not scale easily to higher dimensions (and can become computationally intractable, see [KNS + 19]). It turns out that we can remedy both of these issues by considering elements of X as path segments instead of elements of S M ( R d ) . The pathwise formulation will allow us to employ an array of recently developed powerful tools from rough path theory and (signature-) kernel methods. Write P θ = G θ # U for the push-forward measure of U through G θ . Then, if P is comprised of samples p ∼ P θ , we say the beliefs P are parametric . Else, they are non-parametric .
texts.text: Furthermore, suppose ( U, Σ , U ) is a probability space and G θ : U → C ( I, E ) is a generator with θ ∈ Θ , and Θ ⊂ R d . Write P θ = G θ # U for the push-forward measure of U through G θ . Then, if P is comprised of samples p ∼ P θ , we say the beliefs P are parametric . Else, they are non-parametric .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A natural way to derive beliefs is by setting the generator G θ to be the action of an SDE solver over a given stochastic differential equation parameterized by θ ∈ Θ , which are often calibrated according to observed market data. A less prescriptive, but more computationally expensive, procedure involves modelling beliefs as the output of a generative machine learning model [BHL + 20, NSV + 21, WBWB19, IHLS23]. Here, the generator has been trained on observed market data, synthetic data, or a combination of the two. Note that beliefs obtained from this method could also be prescribed as 'parametric' since the generator architecture is often comprised of highly-parameterized neural networks. Thus these approaches are often called 'data-driven' to distinguish them from the classical approach. Non-parametric beliefs can be obtained by sampling from observed market data.

We formalise this statement with the following.

texts.orig: Problem 1.8 (Market regime clustering problem) . With the notation the same as Problem 1.7, the market regime clustering problem is to find a function F : P ( K ) →{ 1 , . . . , k } which suitably clusters the measures δ 1 , . . . , δ M into C 1 , . . . , C k .
texts.text: Problem 1.8 (Market regime clustering problem) . With the notation the same as Problem 1.7, the market regime clustering problem is to find a function F : P ( K ) →{ 1 , . . . , k } which suitably clusters the measures δ 1 , . . . , δ M into C 1 , . . . , C k .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Problem 1.8 does contain a rather large ambiguity - the definition suitability in regards to the choice of the function F . This is a classic problem in unsupervised learning, as without data labels, goodness-of-fit evaluations are often made either qualitatively or with metrics not used in the learning procedure. Oftentimes the output of a given clustering algorithm can be thought of as assigning a class label to the elements of a given cluster. This approach makes sense if the clusters themselves have sufficient interpretability. Yet, cluster assignments are relative by construction, and more work is required to validate whether the clusters themselves are distinct from each other whilst remaining self-similar . In Section 5, we evaluate our clustering methodology on labelled data so as to verify our class assignments before moving onto unlabelled data.

This approach makes sense if the clusters themselves have sufficient interpretability. Yet, cluster assignments are relative by construction, and more work is required to validate whether the clusters themselves are distinct from each other whilst remaining self-similar . In Section 5, we evaluate our clustering methodology on labelled data so as to verify our class assignments before moving onto unlabelled data.

texts.orig: The first property is the key result which motivates our use of the signature mapping as a way of encoding path data. Again, paths we consider will be linear interpolants of streamed data with an added monotonically increasing channel (corresponding to time), and thus the uniqueness property of S ( X ) [ a,b ] will always hold, as we are working exclusively with tree-like reduced paths. The second property is crucial as it removes dependence on sampling rate, a key consideration when working with financial data which is often available at different tick frequencies. Related to universality of the signature is injectivity of following mapping, which we introduce now.

texts.orig: Often it is advantageous to first transform a stream of data ˆ x ∈ T ∆ ( I, R d ) before calculating its corresponding signature. For example, in financial applications, one is often more concerned with relative changes as opposed to absolute ones, implying a type of path normalisation is necessary when conducting inference with path signatures. In the literature, path transformations have been used to correctly evaluate discrete-time stochastic integrals in the Itô sense [FHL16], directly scale path values [CLX21] or provide sensitivity to translation after applying the signature [MFKL20]. Briefly, the most common choice of transformations used in our experiments are as follows:

texts.orig: 2.3. Inference in reproducing kernel Hilbert spaces. In this section we introduce the basics of inference with kernels. We define the signature kernel (including its higherrank extension) and their associated maximum mean discrepancies (MMD). Finally, we introduce the signature kernel scoring rule, which can be used to perform inference in a set-to-point experimental framework. Again, we leave the majority of the details to Appendix D. For further details on kernel methods we direct the reader to [GBR + 06, GFHS09, GBR + 12].
texts.text: 2.3. Inference in reproducing kernel Hilbert spaces. In this section we introduce the basics of inference with kernels. We define the signature kernel (including its higherrank extension) and their associated maximum mean discrepancies (MMD). Finally, we introduce the signature kernel scoring rule, which can be used to perform inference in a set-to-point experimental framework. Again, we leave the majority of the details to Appendix D. For further details on kernel methods we direct the reader to [GBR + 06, GFHS09, GBR + 12].

texts.orig: 2.3.1. Reproducing kernel Hilbert spaces and the maximum mean discrepancy. We begin this section with a brief introduction to kernels and reproducing kernel Hilbert spaces. In this section, X denotes a non-empty set and H = { f : X → R } a Hilbert space of R -functions on X . In general X is not required to have any structure (it need not even be a topological space). The following quantity does give us a notion of distance between Borel measures on X .

(11) is computationally feasible. We begin with a fundamental definition.

texts.orig: 2.3.2. The two sample test, and consistency. In the literature, the MMD has been used in the context of change-point detection [ACH12, SGK12], minimum distance estimation [BBDG19, CAA22], and generative modelling [LCC + 17, MN21]. Each of these implementations utilizes the power of the MMD as a generalized two-sample test between empirical measures.
texts.text: 2.3.2. The two sample test, and consistency. In the literature, the MMD has been used in the context of change-point detection [ACH12, SGK12], minimum distance estimation [BBDG19, CAA22], and generative modelling [LCC + 17, MN21]. Each of these implementations utilizes the power of the MMD as a generalized two-sample test between empirical measures.

texts.orig: Definition 2.14 (MMD two sample test) . Suppose X = ( x 1 , . . . , x n ) , x i ∼ P for i = 1 , . . . , n and Y = ( y 1 , . . . , y m ) , y j ∼ Q for j = 1 , . . . , m are i.i.d. Then, the MMD twosample test problem is to design an appropriate threshold c α such that if D κ ( X,Y ) > c α , one can reject the null hypothesis H 0 that P = Q at a confidence level α .
texts.text: Definition 2.14 (MMD two sample test) . Suppose X = ( x 1 , . . . , x n ) , x i ∼ P for i = 1 , . . . , n and Y = ( y 1 , . . . , y m ) , y j ∼ Q for j = 1 , . . . , m are i.i.d. Then, the MMD twosample test problem is to design an appropriate threshold c α such that if D κ ( X,Y ) > c α , one can reject the null hypothesis H 0 that P = Q at a confidence level α .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Remark 2.15 . Regarding the i.i.d. assumption: when studying (real) path data, or in certain synthetic cases, one cannot reasonably assume that successive samples are independent of each other. We refer the reader to [CAA22], Section 3 for results regarding the convergence in probability of the sample MMD to the population MMD in the case that sames are not strictly independent. In particular, Theorem 3.2. gives a (distributionfree) bound which uses a version of McDiarmind's [M + 89] inequality to bound the rate of convergence in probability in the case where samples are dependent.
texts.text: Remark 2.15 . Regarding the i.i.d. assumption: when studying (real) path data, or in certain synthetic cases, one cannot reasonably assume that successive samples are independent of each other. We refer the reader to [CAA22], Section 3 for results regarding the convergence in probability of the sample MMD to the population MMD in the case that sames are not strictly independent. In particular, Theorem 3.2. gives a (distributionfree) bound which uses a version of McDiarmind's [M + 89] inequality to bound the rate of convergence in probability in the case where samples are dependent.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A consistent two-sample test achieves a Type I error of α (rejecting H 0 when it is true) and an asymptotic Type II error (accepting H 0 when P = Q ) of 0 . It was shown in [GBR + 12], Theorem 7 that empirical (sample) MMD converges to the population MMD at a rate O (( m + n ) -1 / 2 ) , where n, m ∈ N are the number of samples comprising X and Y respectively. As a consequence, a test at the level (1 -α ) for the biased empirical statistic has associated distribution-free bound √ 2 K/n (1 + 2 √ log α -1 ) (when n = m ). However, this bound is much too conservative for sample sizes used in practice, and data-dependent bounds are often required to achieve a powerful test (and a less conservative c α ).

The most useful for our purposes involves estimating the null distribution via a Gamma approximation, which is O ( n 2 ) to calculate in the number of empirical samples n . However, in our setting we are often confined to using very few samples and thus a simple bootstrapping technique is often sufficient. We outline further in Section 3.3.

Given that S can be thought of as the canonical feature map on path space, it makes sense to define a kernel k ( · , x ) = S ( x ) through S in the following manner.

As of writing, until recently there has not been an explicit kernel trick associated to k sig . Instead, the truncated signature kernel k N sig ( x, y ) = ⟨ S N ( x ) , S N ( y ) ⟩ T N ( V ) was used as an approximation to the true value of k sig ( x, y ) . This technique proved popular, motivated by the factorial decay property exhibited by terms in the signature, see eq. (42) in the Appendix.

texts.orig: Finally, as noted in [CO18], Section 5.5, one may wish to lift paths evolving in a general topological space T to those in a linear space V via a feature map φ : T → V . Such transformations form the fundamental basis for kernel methods and are known to assist the process of learning non-linear relationships on sets of data. In our experiments we often make use of the following lifted kernel, corresponding to φ being the canonical feature map associated to the radial basis function (RBF) kernel on R d .

In our experiments we often make use of the following lifted kernel, corresponding to φ being the canonical feature map associated to the radial basis function (RBF) kernel on R d .

texts.orig: In the final part of this section, we briefly introduce the concept of the higher-rank signature and the associated higher-rank signature kernel . The majority of the details can be found in the Appendix C, and further specifics can be found in [SLL + 21]. We summarize the main ideas here. From Theorem 2.6, given a filtered probability space (Ω , F , ( F t ) t ∈ I , P ) and a F -adapted stochastic process X , the expected signature of X is enough to characterize its law L ( X ) . It turns out that this is not the case for conditional distributions of X with respect to F t . For a filtered process X = (Ω , F , ( F t ) t ∈ I , P , X ) , the object we are interested in studying is the prediction process
texts.text: In the final part of this section, we briefly introduce the concept of the higher-rank signature and the associated higher-rank signature kernel . The majority of the details can be found in the Appendix C, and further specifics can be found in [SLL + 21]. We summarize the main ideas here. From Theorem 2.6, given a filtered probability space (Ω , F , ( F t ) t ∈ I , P ) and a F -adapted stochastic process X , the expected signature of X is enough to characterize its law L ( X ) . It turns out that this is not the case for conditional distributions of X with respect to F t . For a filtered process X = (Ω , F , ( F t ) t ∈ I , P , X ) , the object we are interested in studying is the prediction process

texts.label: text
texts.orig: or the distribution of X conditional on F t at time t ∈ I . One can repeat this process of iterated conditioning to build a prediction process of the prediction process, and so on. The number of iterates determines the rank of the prediction process. We call eq. (20) the rank 1 adapted process with associated law L ( ˆ X 1 ) . The classical law of X can be identified with the rank 0 adapted process, L ( X ) = L ( ˆ X 0 ) . For two filtered processes X , Y , we write X ∼ r Y if L ( ˆ X r ) = L ( ˆ Y r ) , and X ∼ Y if X ∼ r Y for all r ≥ 0 . Again one can associate the classical (weak) equality in law between two stochastic processes X,Y with X ∼ 0 Y .
texts.text: or the distribution of X conditional on F t at time t ∈ I . One can repeat this process of iterated conditioning to build a prediction process of the prediction process, and so on. The number of iterates determines the rank of the prediction process. We call eq. (20) the rank 1 adapted process with associated law L ( ˆ X 1 ) . The classical law of X can be identified with the rank 0 adapted process, L ( X ) = L ( ˆ X 0 ) . For two filtered processes X , Y , we write X ∼ r Y if L ( ˆ X r ) = L ( ˆ Y r ) , and X ∼ Y if X ∼ r Y for all r ≥ 0 . Again one can associate the classical (weak) equality in law between two stochastic processes X,Y with X ∼ 0 Y .

texts.orig: In this way, the rank 2 signature kernel MMD D 2 sig can be used in a two-sample testing framework if we wish to incorporate filtration information. In fact, it follows that if D 2 sig ( X,Y ) = 0 , one must have that D 1 sig ( X,Y ) = 0 ([SLL + 21], Theorem 2), making the rank 2 signature kernel MMD a strictly better metric to use when considering compactly supported measures on path space. The main drawback (which will be shown in Subsection 4.5) is the computational time associated to the rank 2 MMD is some levels of magnitude greater than its rank 1 equivalent.

texts.orig: Definition 2.22 (Scoring rule, [GR07]) . Let P be a convex class of measures on a probability space ( X , A ) . A scoring rule s : P ×X → [ -∞ , ∞ ] is any function such that s ( P , · ) is P -quasi integrable for all P ∈ P .
texts.text: Definition 2.22 (Scoring rule, [GR07]) . Let P be a convex class of measures on a probability space ( X , A ) . A scoring rule s : P ×X → [ -∞ , ∞ ] is any function such that s ( P , · ) is P -quasi integrable for all P ∈ P .

texts.orig: Definition 2.23 (Properness, [GR07]) . A scoring rule s : P × X → [ -∞ , + ∞ ] is called proper (relative to the class P ) if s ( P , P ) ≤ s ( Q , P ) for all P , Q ∈ P . It is called strictly proper if Q = P is the unique minimiser.
texts.text: Definition 2.23 (Properness, [GR07]) . A scoring rule s : P × X → [ -∞ , + ∞ ] is called proper (relative to the class P ) if s ( P , P ) ≤ s ( Q , P ) for all P , Q ∈ P . It is called strictly proper if Q = P is the unique minimiser.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If a scoring rule is strictly proper, one can define a statistical divergence D ( P || Q ) = s ( Q , P ) -s ( P , P ) . For a general strictly proper scoring rule, this divergence is not guaranteed to be a metric - it is not even necessarily symmetric. However, we will focus on a special class of scores where this is indeed the case.
texts.text: If a scoring rule is strictly proper, one can define a statistical divergence D ( P || Q ) = s ( Q , P ) -s ( P , P ) . For a general strictly proper scoring rule, this divergence is not guaranteed to be a metric - it is not even necessarily symmetric. However, we will focus on a special class of scores where this is indeed the case.

texts.orig: 3.1. Partitioning of path data. In this section we outline the data preprocessing procedure required to perform our experiments. In what follows we suppose ˆ s ∈ T ∆ ( I, R d ) is a time-augmented d -dimensional stream of data over an interval [0 , T ] . For simplicity we assume this is representing (close) prices of d fi nancial instruments. We note here that our methodology is not restricted to just prices: in theory, one could incorporate any data deemed relevant to the regime detection or classification problem at hand.

texts.orig: Remark 3.4 (Choice of h ) . We note here that the choice of h = ( h 1 , h 2 ) is largely dependent on the characteristics of the data one is working with. The choice of h 1 , for instance, might be limited due to the frequency of available tick data. In general, the lower the value of h 1 (assuming all price data is equally spaced), the more reactive the detection method will be, at the cost of increased sensitivity to noise. In [MFKL20], several partitionings based on hierarchical dyadic windowing was suggested, which can be a useful framework for determining robustness to scale of the detection and clustering algorithms. Finally we note that in theory h 1 need not be constant: paths can be comprised of many evaluations as long as the period between the first and last observation remains the same.

texts.orig: Regarding the parameter h 2 determining the size of the path ensembles: larger values reduce the variance of the associated estimator, at the cost of being less reactive. New, potentially anomalous samples form a lesser portion of the evaluated ensemble, meaning that short-lived regime 'spikes' may not be captured if they persist for only a short period of time. Again, the choice of h 2 is dependent on the modeller's preference.

texts.orig: 3.2.1. k -class detection with prior beliefs. In this section, we outline our experimental methods in the context of the MRDP. In particular, given ˆ s ∈ T ∆ ([0 , T ]; R d ) , h ∈ N 2 and a stream transformer Φ : S ( R d +1 ) →S ( R p ) , we give details as to how one evaluates elements drawn from the set of (transformed) ensemble paths EP Φ (ˆ s ) .
texts.text: 3.2.1. k -class detection with prior beliefs. In this section, we outline our experimental methods in the context of the MRDP. In particular, given ˆ s ∈ T ∆ ([0 , T ]; R d ) , h ∈ N 2 and a stream transformer Φ : S ( R d +1 ) →S ( R p ) , we give details as to how one evaluates elements drawn from the set of (transformed) ensemble paths EP Φ (ˆ s ) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The first case we explore is where the modeller holds (parametric) beliefs P = ( P 1 , . . . , P k ) . In this setting, each belief class P i is represented via a model P i with associated parameter vector θ i ∈ R d i for i = 1 , . . . , k . We call ( P i , θ i ) a model pair , and sometimes write P θ i .
texts.text: The first case we explore is where the modeller holds (parametric) beliefs P = ( P 1 , . . . , P k ) . In this setting, each belief class P i is represented via a model P i with associated parameter vector θ i ∈ R d i for i = 1 , . . . , k . We call ( P i , θ i ) a model pair , and sometimes write P θ i .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For each model pair, we construct P i by simulating N ∈ N time-augmented paths (denoted by x , for consistency of notation with Definition 3.1) of length h 1 over the interval [0 , T ∗ ] , where T ∗ = Th 1 | ∆ | . Choosing T ∗ in this way ensures that the time-step used to simulate paths p ∼ ( P , θ ) corresponds to that from ∆ . Naturally we are also required to apply the same stream transformer Φ over each path in x i ∈ P i in the same way that we did to the set of sub paths. In practice, this means that each P i is represented as a ( N × h 1 × d ′ ) tensor, and P is thus a ( k × N × h 1 × d ′ ) tensor.

Choosing T ∗ in this way ensures that the time-step used to simulate paths p ∼ ( P , θ ) corresponds to that from ∆ . Naturally we are also required to apply the same stream transformer Φ over each path in x i ∈ P i in the same way that we did to the set of sub paths. In practice, this means that each P i is represented as a ( N × h 1 × d ′ ) tensor, and P is thus a ( k × N × h 1 × d ′ ) tensor.

If there exists at least one c α j such that α ( x i ) j < c α j , then we can claim that the path ensemble x i is not anomalous for our given confidence level. If we wish to make a statement about k -class conformity, we can obtain the quantile value of the score α ( x i ) j by applying the appropriate quantile function F -1 D j : R → [0 , 1] ; lower values are indicative of higher degrees of conformance to beliefs P j .
texts.text: Thus, real-time detection is a matter of comparing the most recent score vector value to the vector of critical values c α = ( c 1 α , . . . , c k α ) . If there exists at least one c α j such that α ( x i ) j < c α j , then we can claim that the path ensemble x i is not anomalous for our given confidence level. If we wish to make a statement about k -class conformity, we can obtain the quantile value of the score α ( x i ) j by applying the appropriate quantile function F -1 D j : R → [0 , 1] ; lower values are indicative of higher degrees of conformance to beliefs P j .

texts.orig: Remark 3.8 . The response time of the regime detector is the amount of time required to pass til a new observation s ∈ SP h (ˆ s ) is observed. For simplicity in this work, we do not discuss these modifications.

texts.orig: 3.2.3. Switching at random times. The first experiment we are interested in is a study of the MRDP in the case where regime changes happen at random times ( τ i ) i M =0 over an interval I = [0 , T ] for T > 0 . This experiment can be performed on both real and synthetic data; here, we will work within the context of synthetic data, where we explicitly know the regime-switching times, before extending the approach to real data, which can be found in Section 6.
texts.text: 3.2.3. Switching at random times. The first experiment we are interested in is a study of the MRDP in the case where regime changes happen at random times ( τ i ) i M =0 over an interval I = [0 , T ] for T > 0 . This experiment can be performed on both real and synthetic data; here, we will work within the context of synthetic data, where we explicitly know the regime-switching times, before extending the approach to real data, which can be found in Section 6.

texts.orig: where s 0 = 1 . First, we specify how the sequence of stopping times ( τ i ) i M =0 are generated. This can be done in one of two ways. The first is by randomly sampling points from ∆ and prescribing the regime change persists for a fixed period of time a ∈ R + . In this way T i = [ τ i , τ i + a ) and we have that τ i = τ i -1 + a , except for the last interval T M = [ τ M , T ] .
texts.text: where s 0 = 1 . First, we specify how the sequence of stopping times ( τ i ) i M =0 are generated. This can be done in one of two ways. The first is by randomly sampling points from ∆ and prescribing the regime change persists for a fixed period of time a ∈ R + . In this way T i = [ τ i , τ i + a ) and we have that τ i = τ i -1 + a , except for the last interval T M = [ τ M , T ] .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Else, we allow the regime change (i.e., the length of T i ) to persist for a random period of time. We iterate over each point on the mesh ∆ in the following way: we start at t = 0 with model dynamics P θ 1 . Then, after h 1 time points, we first draw a random variable Z 1 ∼ Po( λ 1 ) which tells us if we change regime or not. If Z 2 > 0 , then we set τ i := t k and flag that we are in a regime change. We then simulate the path until we reach the next time-step that is a multiple of h 1 , plus one. If we are not in a regime change then we draw Z 1 ∼ Po( λ 1 ) again; otherwise, simulate a second random variable Z 2 ∼ Po( λ 2 ) which tells us whether we exit the regime change or not. If Z 2 > 0 we set τ i +1 := t k + h 1 +1 ; otherwise, we move to the next time step. We end up with a sequence of times ( τ i ) i ≥ 0 whereby | τ i -τ i -1 | is a random variable. Moreover, the number of times is also random. If the number of regime changes is greater than M , we simply begin with P θ 1 again.
texts.text: Else, we allow the regime change (i.e., the length of T i ) to persist for a random period of time. We iterate over each point on the mesh ∆ in the following way: we start at t = 0 with model dynamics P θ 1 . Then, after h 1 time points, we first draw a random variable Z 1 ∼ Po( λ 1 ) which tells us if we change regime or not. If Z 2 > 0 , then we set τ i := t k and flag that we are in a regime change. We then simulate the path until we reach the next time-step that is a multiple of h 1 , plus one. If we are not in a regime change then we draw Z 1 ∼ Po( λ 1 ) again; otherwise, simulate a second random variable Z 2 ∼ Po( λ 2 ) which tells us whether we exit the regime change or not. If Z 2 > 0 we set τ i +1 := t k + h 1 +1 ; otherwise, we move to the next time step. We end up with a sequence of times ( τ i ) i ≥ 0 whereby | τ i -τ i -1 | is a random variable. Moreover, the number of times is also random. If the number of regime changes is greater than M , we simply begin with P θ 1 again.

texts.orig: Regarding evaluating elements of s i ∈ EP Φ h (ˆ s ) for i = 1 , . . . , N 2 : in the parametric (beliefs) case, after specifying P = ( P 1 , . . . , P k ) we first build the associated empirical distributions D = ( D 1 , . . . , D k ) and obtain the vector of (1 -α )% quantiles c α = ( c 1 α , . . . , c k α ) via the estimation or bootstrapping method. Then, for a given number of evaluations n ∈ N we progressively calculate the score matrix from eq. (28) for a given number of evaluations n ∈ N . Detection is then a matter of comparing elements of α ( s i ) with their corresponding (1 -α )% critical values. For j = 1 , . . . , k , if α ( s i ) j < c α j , then the path collection s i conforms to the model pair represented by the beliefs P j . If α ( s i ) fails to conform to any of our beliefs, then we can interpret the given path segment as being anomalous.

texts.orig: In the non-parametric case, we calculate the (weighted) L -lag auto evaluation score (29) and report this. Theory tells us that the MMD score between path ensembles drawn from the same distribution will be asymptotically 0 , and conform to a null distribution for a given number of finite samples. During a regime change, newly distributed samples enter the most recent ensemble, causing the MMD score to become larger relative to previous values. It will then decay to a lower level as each path ensemble again contains paths drawn from the new regime.

During a regime change, newly distributed samples enter the most recent ensemble, causing the MMD score to become larger relative to previous values. It will then decay to a lower level as each path ensemble again contains paths drawn from the new regime.

texts.orig: 3.2.4. Non-ensemble evaluation. In this section, we give an experimental methodology for the market regime detection problem in the case where only a single path sample is to be evaluated. That is, we are interested in performing regime detection over the set of transformed sub paths SP Φ h (ˆ s ) where ˆ s is a time-augmented discrete asset price path. As we cannot directly apply the MMD in this situation, we instead seek to use the rankr signature kernel scoring rule s k r sig as a way of detecting conformance from paths s ∈ SP Φ h (ˆ s ) to our beliefs P . As we are not able to directly apply detection based on twosample testing, we require a slightly different approach which makes use of the following function.

texts.orig: 3.3. A method for the market regime classification problem. Suppose ( E,d ) is a metric space and suppose s ∈ S ( E ) is a stream of data over E comprised of N ∈ N elements. The problem of grouping data points x i ∈ X,i = 1 , . . . , N into a fixed number of partitions C l , l ∈ N such that elements of each C l share similar characteristics (usually defined under d ) is referred to as clustering the vector X . This process is a classic unsupervised learning technique which has many popular approaches: k -means [Mac65], k -medoids [KR09], fuzzy clustering and its relatives [Dun73] or density-based models such as DBSCAN [Lin72]. Each of these methods has their own benefits and drawbacks, which depending on the context make some algorithms more suitable than others. We refer the reader to [HIM21] for a more in-depth summary of various clustering techniques and in particular their application to mathematical finance and regime classification.

This process is a classic unsupervised learning technique which has many popular approaches: k -means [Mac65], k -medoids [KR09], fuzzy clustering and its relatives [Dun73] or density-based models such as DBSCAN [Lin72]. Each of these methods has their own benefits and drawbacks, which depending on the context make some algorithms more suitable than others. We refer the reader to [HIM21] for a more in-depth summary of various clustering techniques and in particular their application to mathematical finance and regime classification.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Another classical technique is called hierarchical clustering , first given in [Joh67]. The algorithm employs either a top-down (divisive) or bottom-up (agglomerative) approach to partitioning data into k ∈ N distinct clusters where k is chosen by the user. In the top-down approach, data is assumed to belong to one cluster, whereas the agglomerative approach assumes the opposite: all values x i ∈ X belong to their own cluster and are progressively agglomerated. In both cases, the algorithm requires a pairwise distance matrix D ∈ R N × N , with elements given by

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 3.4. Overview of experiments. The rest of the paper is devoted to experiments showing how one can successfully apply the different versions of the MMD 6 (truncated MMD, kernel MMD, and in particular the higher-rank MMD which is our method of choice), and the signature kernel scoring rule, to regime detection and classification problems on path space. Here we will briefly summarize each of the experiments and in particular how we evaluate the performance of our approach.
texts.text: 3.4. Overview of experiments. The rest of the paper is devoted to experiments showing how one can successfully apply the different versions of the MMD 6 (truncated MMD, kernel MMD, and in particular the higher-rank MMD which is our method of choice), and the signature kernel scoring rule, to regime detection and classification problems on path space. Here we will briefly summarize each of the experiments and in particular how we evaluate the performance of our approach.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Section 4 is devoted to the market regime detection problem and its variants. In the entire section we focus synthetically generated data, before moving on to real data experiments in Section 6. We do this separation so that we can first establish the accuracy and validity of our methods, by evaluating our algorithms output against known (synthetic) solutions. Subsection 4.1 showcases our approach on a first simple toy example that can be easily validated: There we consider a regime-switching problem where the underlying process is a univariate geometric Brownian motion (gBm) and the different regimes correspond to different parameter choices, which are flipped at random times. Within this framework we first provide examples of experiments in the case where the modeller holds a given (parametric) belief P , and then proceed to case where the modeller holds a priori no beliefs, but infers these from observations of the market in an evolving manner (this is done by evaluating the observed path against itself as per Definition 3.9). This first experiment demonstrates that regime changes at random times can be detected with a high confidence.
texts.text: Section 4 is devoted to the market regime detection problem and its variants. In the entire section we focus synthetically generated data, before moving on to real data experiments in Section 6. We do this separation so that we can first establish the accuracy and validity of our methods, by evaluating our algorithms output against known (synthetic) solutions. Subsection 4.1 showcases our approach on a first simple toy example that can be easily validated: There we consider a regime-switching problem where the underlying process is a univariate geometric Brownian motion (gBm) and the different regimes correspond to different parameter choices, which are flipped at random times. Within this framework we first provide examples of experiments in the case where the modeller holds a given (parametric) belief P , and then proceed to case where the modeller holds a priori no beliefs, but infers these from observations of the market in an evolving manner (this is done by evaluating the observed path against itself as per Definition 3.9). This first experiment demonstrates that regime changes at random times can be detected with a high confidence.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In order do add further information about direction and magnitude of such changes we consider a case of multiclass prior beliefs which is demonstrated in Subsection 4.2. Here we extend this first toy example to the modeller holding multiple beliefs P = ( P 1 , . . . , P k ) to add granularity about the direction and magnitude of changes.
texts.text: In order do add further information about direction and magnitude of such changes we consider a case of multiclass prior beliefs which is demonstrated in Subsection 4.2. Here we extend this first toy example to the modeller holding multiple beliefs P = ( P 1 , . . . , P k ) to add granularity about the direction and magnitude of changes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: An extreme case of the the first simple toy example would be if we want to detect regime changes based on single-path observations. To demonstrate that this is possible in our framework, we demonstrate in Subsection 4.3 an experiment, that takes as a starting point the first motivating toy example. However, in this new setting we restrict the detection problem to a path-by-path setting, as opposed to using ensembles. Initial experiments have shown that naive methods (such as truncation) may struggle to yield reliable detection performance on non-Markovian data, therefore we show that our method is designed to work for such data as well. In order to showcase that our method also works on data that exhibits an autocorrelative structure and non-Markovian properties, we demonstrate experiments on synthetic data generated by rough stochastic volatility models in Subsection 4.4. Within the same section also demonstrate in passing how the detection problem can be extended to multidimensional processes.

However, in this new setting we restrict the detection problem to a path-by-path setting, as opposed to using ensembles. Initial experiments have shown that naive methods (such as truncation) may struggle to yield reliable detection performance on non-Markovian data, therefore we show that our method is designed to work for such data as well. In order to showcase that our method also works on data that exhibits an autocorrelative structure and non-Markovian properties, we demonstrate experiments on synthetic data generated by rough stochastic volatility models in Subsection 4.4. Within the same section also demonstrate in passing how the detection problem can be extended to multidimensional processes.
