texts.orig: Subsection 4.5 shows how utilizing the higher-rank signature kernel MMD method can lead to better accuracy in regime detection (than its lower rank counterparts), however this comes at a computational cost and we conclude the section by leaving it to the modeller to choose the method according to her preferences between accuracy and speed of the method. Subsection 4.6 showcases how our method can be applied as an anomaly detection tool. We benchmark our technique against an existing signature-based method for anomaly detection developed in [CFLA20]. Finally, Subsection 4.7 gives an experiment where non-Markovianity of sample trajectories is handled via the similarity score function from Definition 3.12.
texts.text: Subsection 4.5 shows how utilizing the higher-rank signature kernel MMD method can lead to better accuracy in regime detection (than its lower rank counterparts), however this comes at a computational cost and we conclude the section by leaving it to the modeller to choose the method according to her preferences between accuracy and speed of the method. Subsection 4.6 showcases how our method can be applied as an anomaly detection tool. We benchmark our technique against an existing signature-based method for anomaly detection developed in [CFLA20]. Finally, Subsection 4.7 gives an experiment where non-Markovianity of sample trajectories is handled via the similarity score function from Definition 3.12.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Section 5 is concerned with the market regime classification problem. In contrast to the previous section, here we are interested in finding regimes on path space via clustering (ensembles of) paths extracted from a (simulated) path observation. Again, we focus on synthetic data examples in the entire section and refer to Section 6 for real data experiments. As before, the starting point is a simple gBm example with two regimes (that change at random times), shown in Section 5. We draw the comparison of this regime classification method to our previous results (see [HIM21]) and demonstrate how our kernel-based method helps go beyond dimensionality limitations that we were facing in the returns-based setting, previously in [HIM21]. For this we compare three clustering methods against each other: the Wasserstein k -means approach explored in [HIM21], a naive truncated-signature-based algorithm, and our kernel-based clustering, see Figure 16. Specifically, within a high-dimensional setting, we show how our kernel-based method extends the other two considered clustering methods, and in particular, outperforms the truncated signature-MMD based method.
texts.text: Section 5 is concerned with the market regime classification problem. In contrast to the previous section, here we are interested in finding regimes on path space via clustering (ensembles of) paths extracted from a (simulated) path observation. Again, we focus on synthetic data examples in the entire section and refer to Section 6 for real data experiments. As before, the starting point is a simple gBm example with two regimes (that change at random times), shown in Section 5. We draw the comparison of this regime classification method to our previous results (see [HIM21]) and demonstrate how our kernel-based method helps go beyond dimensionality limitations that we were facing in the returns-based setting, previously in [HIM21]. For this we compare three clustering methods against each other: the Wasserstein k -means approach explored in [HIM21], a naive truncated-signature-based algorithm, and our kernel-based clustering, see Figure 16. Specifically, within a high-dimensional setting, we show how our kernel-based method extends the other two considered clustering methods, and in particular, outperforms the truncated signature-MMD based method.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Finally, after establishing the validity of both our MRDP and MRCP methods on verifiable synthetic data in the previous two Sections (4 and 5), we demonstrate the performance of our method on a variety of real data examples in the final Section 6. The real data examples we consider are organised as follows: Section 6.1 explores both the MRDP and MRCP for a multidimensional setting comprised of a basket of equities. We verify that our methodologies successfully identify periods of known historical market turmoils (such as the recent coronavirus pandemic or the dot-com crisis). We also observe that the obtained MMD scores tracks the VIX fairly accurately, which further highlights the the speed of detection ability at a good accuracy.
texts.text: Finally, after establishing the validity of both our MRDP and MRCP methods on verifiable synthetic data in the previous two Sections (4 and 5), we demonstrate the performance of our method on a variety of real data examples in the final Section 6. The real data examples we consider are organised as follows: Section 6.1 explores both the MRDP and MRCP for a multidimensional setting comprised of a basket of equities. We verify that our methodologies successfully identify periods of known historical market turmoils (such as the recent coronavirus pandemic or the dot-com crisis). We also observe that the obtained MMD scores tracks the VIX fairly accurately, which further highlights the the speed of detection ability at a good accuracy.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Subsection 6.2 applies our regime detection- and regime classification methods on (multidimensional) cryptocurrency data. Again, known periods of instability are tracked efficiently and accurately though our MMD scores even in high-dimensional cases. It is worth noting that accuracy is not compromised by higher dimensional settings, which is expected in our methodological design.
texts.text: Subsection 6.2 applies our regime detection- and regime classification methods on (multidimensional) cryptocurrency data. Again, known periods of instability are tracked efficiently and accurately though our MMD scores even in high-dimensional cases. It is worth noting that accuracy is not compromised by higher dimensional settings, which is expected in our methodological design.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Finally, Subsection 6.3 showcases again an extreme example in a single-path-observation setting: This example demonstrates how our MRD and MRC methodologies can be combined in a setting where the practitioner holds non-parametric beliefs, to create a wholly data-driven detection pipeline, evaluated (path-by-path) on single-observations.
texts.text: Finally, Subsection 6.3 showcases again an extreme example in a single-path-observation setting: This example demonstrates how our MRD and MRC methodologies can be combined in a setting where the practitioner holds non-parametric beliefs, to create a wholly data-driven detection pipeline, evaluated (path-by-path) on single-observations.

texts.orig: We begin by describing how we generate the regime-switching path. For simplicity here, we assume only two possible regimes: either a low or high volatility period. This defines our sequence of model pairs M = ( P θ 1 , P θ 2 ) , with θ 1 = (0 , 0 . 2) and θ 2 = (0 , 0 . 3) . For a given T > 0 and partition ∆ of [0 , T ] , we generate a path ˆ s ∈ T ∆ ([0 , T ] , R ) where at the random times τ 1 , . . . , τ M regime dynamics cycle between elements of M as outlined in Section 3.2. Initially we take T = 4 and set | ∆ | = 1 252 × 7 , so each path value roughly represents hourly tick data. Our regime switching times are determined by the random variable Z 1 ∼ Po(2) and regime exit times by Z 2 ∼ Po(1 / 49) . An example of such a regime-changed path is given in Figure 2 and the corresponding path of log-returns is given alongside it.

texts.orig: Regarding the choice of metric, the rank 1 MMD is appropriate for this example due to Theorem C.5. Utilizing a higher-rank version of the MMD would incur increased computational cost for marginal improvements in inference. Finally, we applied the RBF lift in the calculation of the signature kernel and chose the smoothing hyperparameter σ = 0 . 025 . The associated maximum mean discrepancy is given by eq. (21). We determined σ via a bootstrapping technique and note here that the process of determining an optimal scaling in a finite-sample setting is the topic of current research.

texts.orig: We take a moment here to explain the plots shown in Figure 3 which we will continue to display over the course of the paper. In Figure 3a we have the same regime-changed path from Figure 2a. Green shading indicates that section of the path was generated according to P θ 1 , and red shading corresponds to P θ 2 . In grey we have the score matrix Λ(ˆ s ) from eq. (28) which in this case (as we have just one set of beliefs) is the vector ( α ( s i )) N 2 i =1 . Figure 3b quantifies the degree of regime change by individually plotting each element s ∈ SP h (ˆ s ) . The intensity of shading is given by the percentage of the time a given path s was part of an ensemble s ∈ EP h (ˆ s ) such that α ( s ) > c α , i.e., part of an ensemble such that the two-sample test concluded that s is distributed differently to the underlying beliefs of the modeller.

texts.orig: 4.2. Multiclass beliefs. In most instances, holding one set of parametric beliefs will not provide the modeller a descriptive enough picture of the componentry surrounding a regime change, should one occur. In practice one might choose to hold multiple beliefs P = ( P 1 , . . . , P k ) and evaluate observations against each of these in order to characterize a regime change should one occur.
texts.text: 4.2. Multiclass beliefs. In practice one might choose to hold multiple beliefs P = ( P 1 , . . . , P k ) and evaluate observations against each of these in order to characterize a regime change should one occur.

texts.orig: The MMD scores evolve as one expects: in the lowest volatility case, they increase during each successive regime change, and the reverse is true for the belief corresponding to the highest volatility. The middle 'base case' belief exhibits a U-shaped MMD score as one would expect. This illustrates how holding multiple beliefs can be useful in determining directional change in regimes. According to the first two beliefs, the latter intervals constitute a regime change, though it may be initially unclear as to what that regime change represents. By including a high-volatility belief, we can see that the associated score vector during these latter intervals is low, characteristic the corresponding regime.

texts.orig: 4.3. Single path (non-ensemble) evaluation. In this section we present a toy example where online regime detection can be performed on a path-by-path basis, as opposed to ensembles of paths against ensembles of paths. This may be required in instances where data of the desired structure is scarce, or a more sensitive detection algorithm is required.
texts.text: 4.3. Single path (non-ensemble) evaluation. In this section we present a toy example where online regime detection can be performed on a path-by-path basis, as opposed to ensembles of paths against ensembles of paths. This may be required in instances where data of the desired structure is scarce, or a more sensitive detection algorithm is required.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In our framework, for a given path ˆ s ∈ T ∆ ([0 , T ]; R d ) , single path evaluation requires working successively on the space of sub-paths SP (ˆ s ) as opposed to EP h (ˆ s ) . Thus we require a function that operates directly on SP h (ˆ s ) . This necessitates the use of the similarity score Σ P , Q (cf Def. 3.12) where P , Q are distributions on path space. Recall that Σ P , Q gives a measure of conformance a path s ∈ SP h (ˆ s ) has to either P or Q . Negative values indicate greater conformance to P ; positive values indicate greater conformance to Q .

texts.orig: Eq. (38) was introduced in [BFG16] and is called the rough Bergomi (rBergomi) model . It is parametrized by the vector θ = ( ξ 0 , ν, ρ, H ) , where ξ 0 is the initial (forward) variance, ν controls the level of the at-the-money (ATM) skew of the associated volatility surface, ρ the correlation between volatility and price moves, and H the Hurst exponent governing the roughness of the volatility process.

texts.orig: We process a given regime-changed path ˆ s v by choosing h = (16 , 8) in order to generate SP h (ˆ s v ) . We take the path transformer Φ = ϕ norm ◦ ϕ time to obtain SP Φ h (ˆ s v ) and thus EP Φ h (ˆ s v ) . As filtration information is relevant, we choose our evaluation metric to be the rank 1 MMD D 1 sig with associated RBF kernel smoothing hyperparameter σ 1 = 0 . 025 . A sample result is given in Figure 9, where one can visually identify the MMD score increasing during periods of regime change and decreasing when the path returns to conforming to our specified beliefs. The threshold plot confirms our visual intuition, with the MMD score being greater than the prior threshold more often during periods of regime change.

In this way we leverage the fact that we can both perform higherdimensional MMD computations with ease. In what follows we set d = 10 so our regimechanged path ˆ s v ∈ T ∆ ([0 , T ] , R 20 ) is comprised of 10 rBergomi stock price processes with their accompanying volatility processes. Again we choose h = (16 , 8) and our metric is given by D 1 sig , with σ 1 = 0 . 025 .

texts.orig: 4.5. The higher rank MMD. As given in Theorems D.3 and C.5 in the Appendix, we have seen from a simple synthetic example with geometric Brownian motions that the rank 1 MMD is capable of capturing differences between Markovian processes. Due to Theorem 2 in [SLL + 21], we know the rank 2 MMD is capable of distinguishing between such sets of paths as well. From Subsection 4.4, we have also seen that the rank 1 MMD is also able to distinguish between non-Markovian processes X,Y if their marginal distributions are sufficiently different. However, if differences between the marginal distributions of X and Y are more subtle, and if there is some information contained within F X or F Y (or both), then one would expect the regime detector equipped with the rank 2 MMD to outperform that with the rank 1 MMD.
texts.text: 4.5. The higher rank MMD. As given in Theorems D.3 and C.5 in the Appendix, we have seen from a simple synthetic example with geometric Brownian motions that the rank 1 MMD is capable of capturing differences between Markovian processes. Due to Theorem 2 in [SLL + 21], we know the rank 2 MMD is capable of distinguishing between such sets of paths as well. From Subsection 4.4, we have also seen that the rank 1 MMD is also able to distinguish between non-Markovian processes X,Y if their marginal distributions are sufficiently different. However, if differences between the marginal distributions of X and Y are more subtle, and if there is some information contained within F X or F Y (or both), then one would expect the regime detector equipped with the rank 2 MMD to outperform that with the rank 1 MMD.

texts.orig: To illustrate this, we provide an experiment where a regime-switching detection problem with prior beliefs is first performed using the rank-1 MMD, and then with the rank2 MMD associated to the rank-2 signature mapping from Definition C.7. Here our two cycling measures are given by a geometric Brownian motion with parameters θ 1 = (0 , 0 . 2) and an rBergomi model with parameters θ 2 = (0 . 1 , 0 . 1 , -0 . 7 , 0 . 3) . We set our beliefs in both settings to that given by the geometric Brownian motion. The hyperparameters for the rank-1 MMD detector was chosen to be σ = 0 . 5 , where for the rank-2 MMD detector (MMD2-DET), we set σ 1 = 0 . 5 , σ 2 = 1 . . These parameters were determined via bootstrapping. Null distributions D 1 , D 2 were obtained by further bootstrapping with ( h 1 , h 2 ) = (21 , 10) . Regarding the regime-changed path: we set the grid mesh to mirror daily returns, so | ∆ | = 1 / 252 . We simulated a path X ∈ T ∆ ([0 , T ]; R d ) , where here we took d = 5 and T = 20 . Regime changes were given by Z 1 ∼ Po (0 . 25) and exits given by Z 2 ∼ Po (5) .

texts.orig: Figure 11 gives an example run of both detectors on the given switching problem. Although both are somewhat able to distinguish each regime, it is clear that the MMD2DET is better able to identify the regime changes and their subsequent cessations. Again we provide the score plot and the (1 -α )% threshold plots. Finally, Table 1 gives a summary of how each detector performed over n = 100 runs. We directly report the accuracy, which is measured in the following way: for each path s ∈ SP h (ˆ s ) , assign the label 0 if s was generated by P θ 1 , and 1 if it was generated by P θ 2 . For each s ∈ SP h (ˆ s ) , the detector receives a score between 0 and 1 , given by the percentage of the time s belong to an ensemble which failed the two-sample test. We condition the performance in three categories: Regime on, during the standard regime; regime off, during the regime change, and finally the total accuracy. We also report ROC AUC scores (given we hav labelled data) and the total algorithm run time.

texts.orig: 4.6. Comparison to existing techniques. In this section we showcase that our method can be used for anomaly detection in streamed data. We also compare the performance of our algorithm against a existing (signature-based) anomaly detection algorithms and find that it is competitive with existing methods, and even outperforms them in some case.
texts.text: 4.6. Comparison to existing techniques. In this section we showcase that our method can be used for anomaly detection in streamed data. We also compare the performance of our algorithm against a existing (signature-based) anomaly detection algorithms and find that it is competitive with existing methods, and even outperforms them in some case.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As mentioned in Subsection 1.3, the MRDP is closely linked to the classical anomaly detection problem in time series data in the single belief setting. In this section we compare our technique to existing ones in the literature. The most relevant comparison to make is with the techniques given in [CFLA20] which also seeks to perform anomaly detection on path space. We also show how our approach compares to using the truncated signature kernel k N sig and the associated MMD. We reserve the explanation of the signature conformance (SIG-CON) method from [CFLA20] to Appendix F.
texts.text: As mentioned in Subsection 1.3, the MRDP is closely linked to the classical anomaly detection problem in time series data in the single belief setting. In this section we compare our technique to existing ones in the literature. The most relevant comparison to make is with the techniques given in [CFLA20] which also seeks to perform anomaly detection on path space. We also show how our approach compares to using the truncated signature kernel k N sig and the associated MMD. We reserve the explanation of the signature conformance (SIG-CON) method from [CFLA20] to Appendix F.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We outline results of each of the algorithms on an anomaly detection problem. Again, stratified accuracy and ROC AUC scores are reported for both the MMD-DET and the two comparison methods (SIG-CON and the truncated MMD, which we call MMD-T). We note here that our method of assigning accuracy as outlined at the end of Subsection 4.5 is not a perfect like-for-like comparison to the method provided in [CFLA20] as (overall) scores are not assigned 'on the fly' -every ensemble that a given sub-path s ∈ SP h (ˆ s ) was a part of must receive a score before the anomaly score associated to s can be supplied. We argue that any benefits incurred from our experimental setup is an argument for studying path ensembles rather than individual paths in the context of anomaly detection; see [ZGR21] for more details.
texts.text: We outline results of each of the algorithms on an anomaly detection problem. Again, stratified accuracy and ROC AUC scores are reported for both the MMD-DET and the two comparison methods (SIG-CON and the truncated MMD, which we call MMD-T). We note here that our method of assigning accuracy as outlined at the end of Subsection 4.5 is not a perfect like-for-like comparison to the method provided in [CFLA20] as (overall) scores are not assigned 'on the fly' -every ensemble that a given sub-path s ∈ SP h (ˆ s ) was a part of must receive a score before the anomaly score associated to s can be supplied. We argue that any benefits incurred from our experimental setup is an argument for studying path ensembles rather than individual paths in the context of anomaly detection; see [ZGR21] for more details.

texts.orig: The anomaly detection problem we study is again the toy example from Section 4.1, except we study paths of dimensionality d = 5 . We set P θ 1 = (gBm , (0 , 0 . 2)) and P θ 2 = (gBm , (0 , 0 . 3)) and ∆ = { 0 = t 0 < · · · < 4 } where | ∆ | = 7 × 252 . We denote the regime-changed path by ˆ s ∈ T ∆ ([0 , T ]; R 5 ) . Regarding hyperparameter selections: we set h = (8 , 16) , so sub-paths contained 8 observations. We used the rank 1 signature for the detector associated to the signature kernel k sig , and thus the associated metric was given by the rank 1 (RBF-lifted) MMD from eq. (21). For MMD-T, we used the same parameters and truncated the signature mapping at N = 5 . For MMD-DET, we set the smoothing parameter σ = 0 . 0025 . Our beliefs are again distributed according to samples from P θ 1 , and we bootstrap the null distribution D with N 1 = 512 samples.

texts.orig: For the conformance-based method, we also choose the corpus of beliefs to be distributed according to P θ 1 . We truncated the signature at rank N = 2 . Larger levels of truncation were not able to be tested due to memory constraints (highlighting again the issue of directly evaluating the signature map). The null distribution is bootstrapped with | P 1 | = | P 2 | = 1000 paths. All methods had paths first transformed via the stream transformer Φ = ϕ norm ◦ ϕ time .

texts.orig: Visual results are given in Figure 12. By considering path ensembles, the MMD-based anomaly detector gives more 'graded' results than the conformance-based method. This is made clear in the threshold plots from Figures 12d and 12f. Furthermore, it is interesting to note that even with a relatively large level of truncation N = 5 , the truncated MMD is not able to distinguish between regimes at all. This is likely due to the fact that necessary information is embedded in higher-order terms which are not considered by construction. We summarize the results in Table 2.

texts.orig: We conclude this section with an explicit comparison between the true signature kernel k 1 sig and the truncated variant k N sig . We do so by studying a process with jumps, which will introduce increased kurtosis on the marginal distributions of the path. Higher order terms of the signature will be required to effectively detect changes in regime (it would require a signature up to order 4 at the very least), and in theory the MMD associated to k N sig cannot pick up on such differences. We are again in the random-time regime switching problem with two possible states: P θ = gBm( θ ) and P ϕ = MJD( ϕ ) , where MJD (Merton jump diffusion) is the model corresponding to the stochastic differential equation

texts.orig: 4.7. Evaluation of non-Markovian data. Suppose you have a probability space (Ω , F , ( F t ) , P ) and an F t -adapted stochastic process X : [0 , T ] → R . Suppose you observe a sample x ∼ L ( X ) , necessarily in discrete time over a grid ∆ = { 0 = t 0 < t 1 < · · · < t N = T } , so in fact one works with ˆ x ∈ T ∆ ([0 , T ]; R ) . As we have mentioned in Subsection 3.1, we are interested in fusing sub-paths of the stream ˆ x into collections of path ensembles. In a continuous-time setting, ensemble paths can be extracted by first defining a mesh ∆ ′ = { 0 = u 0 < u 1 < · · · < u N = T } and considering the collection of paths X = { x | [ u i -1 ,u i ] : i = 1 , . . . , M } . If | u i -u i -1 | is equal for all i = 1 , . . . , M , then (given certain conditions, which we will discuss) one can set τ := u i -u i -1 and we have that the collection X defines an empirical measure ν on a compact subset K of path space C ([0 , τ ] : R ) where X ⊆ K . In particular we assume all paths are of bounded variation; as mentioned in Subsection 2.1, in our setting this is a fine assumption to make.
texts.text: 4.7. Evaluation of non-Markovian data. Suppose you have a probability space (Ω , F , ( F t ) , P ) and an F t -adapted stochastic process X : [0 , T ] → R . Suppose you observe a sample x ∼ L ( X ) , necessarily in discrete time over a grid ∆ = { 0 = t 0 < t 1 < · · · < t N = T } , so in fact one works with ˆ x ∈ T ∆ ([0 , T ]; R ) . As we have mentioned in Subsection 3.1, we are interested in fusing sub-paths of the stream ˆ x into collections of path ensembles. In a continuous-time setting, ensemble paths can be extracted by first defining a mesh ∆ ′ = { 0 = u 0 < u 1 < · · · < u N = T } and considering the collection of paths X = { x | [ u i -1 ,u i ] : i = 1 , . . . , M } . If | u i -u i -1 | is equal for all i = 1 , . . . , M , then (given certain conditions, which we will discuss) one can set τ := u i -u i -1 and we have that the collection X defines an empirical measure ν on a compact subset K of path space C ([0 , τ ] : R ) where X ⊆ K . In particular we assume all paths are of bounded variation; as mentioned in Subsection 2.1, in our setting this is a fine assumption to make.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In our work, we use D r sig to compare the measure ν generated by X with another measure µ ∈ P ( K ) , usually given by samples drawn from P . In order for the finite-sample (unbiased) estimator associated to D r sig to be asymptotically consistent, one requires that each element x ∈ µ, y ∈ ν is 1) independent and 2) identically distributed (i.i.d), see Definition 2.11. In practice, the generator associated to µ is explicitly known and thus one can indiscriminately draw i.i.d samples from µ .
texts.text: In our work, we use D r sig to compare the measure ν generated by X with another measure µ ∈ P ( K ) , usually given by samples drawn from P . In order for the finite-sample (unbiased) estimator associated to D r sig to be asymptotically consistent, one requires that each element x ∈ µ, y ∈ ν is 1) independent and 2) identically distributed (i.i.d), see Definition 2.11. In practice, the generator associated to µ is explicitly known and thus one can indiscriminately draw i.i.d samples from µ .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Let us now consider the set X , and by extension the measure ν . We want to derive conditions for when we can assume that each element of X could correspond to i.i.d. samples. Even if we assume that all path segments drawn from X have been generated by the same data-generating measure, we still cannot necessary conclude that this is true. To illustrate this, we consider two cases: either the underlying process X is a) Markovian or b) non-Markovian.
texts.text: Let us now consider the set X , and by extension the measure ν . We want to derive conditions for when we can assume that each element of X could correspond to i.i.d. samples. Even if we assume that all path segments drawn from X have been generated by the same data-generating measure, we still cannot necessary conclude that this is true. To illustrate this, we consider two cases: either the underlying process X is a) Markovian or b) non-Markovian.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Recall that a stochastic process X on (Ω , F , ( F t , P ) is Markovian if P ( X t |F s ) = P ( X t | X s ) for all 0 ≤ s ≤ t . Else, it is non-Markovian, which is to say that the transition density of X s to X t depends on the entire history of X up until time s .
texts.text: Recall that a stochastic process X on (Ω , F , ( F t , P ) is Markovian if P ( X t |F s ) = P ( X t | X s ) for all 0 ≤ s ≤ t . Else, it is non-Markovian, which is to say that the transition density of X s to X t depends on the entire history of X up until time s .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Extending the definition of Markovianity to path space, one can see that if X is Markov then the path space measures P ( X | [ s,t ] ∈ ·|F s ) and P ( X | [ s,t ] ∈ ·| X s ) agree. Therefore, the only piece of information required to compare measures on path space where the underlying process x ∼ L ( X ) is Markovian is the initial data x s .
texts.text: Extending the definition of Markovianity to path space, one can see that if X is Markov then the path space measures P ( X | [ s,t ] ∈ ·|F s ) and P ( X | [ s,t ] ∈ ·| X s ) agree. Therefore, the only piece of information required to compare measures on path space where the underlying process x ∼ L ( X ) is Markovian is the initial data x s .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Returning to our original problem, each sample x ∈ X is a priori itself a sample from a conditional distribution: x i ∼ P ( X | [ u i -1 ,u i ] ∈ ·|F X u i -1 ) . If the underlying process is Markovian then it is clear that (after adjusting for initial data) a) each sample is independent (Markov processes are memoryless) and b) they are identically distributed, since
texts.text: Returning to our original problem, each sample x ∈ X is a priori itself a sample from a conditional distribution: x i ∼ P ( X | [ u i -1 ,u i ] ∈ ·|F X u i -1 ) . If the underlying process is Markovian then it is clear that (after adjusting for initial data) a) each sample is independent (Markov processes are memoryless) and b) they are identically distributed, since

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: P ( X | [ u i -1 ,u i ] ∈ ·|F X u i -1 ) = P ( X | [ u i -1 ,u i ] ∈ ·| X u i -1 )

texts.orig: under our initial assumptions. Therefore, by a simple path re-scaling, we can study the processes ˆ x i = x i /x i 0 , which will comply with our stated assumptions.
texts.text: under our initial assumptions. Therefore, by a simple path re-scaling, we can study the processes ˆ x i = x i /x i 0 , which will comply with our stated assumptions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Obviously if X is not Markovian then the preceding analysis fails. In this case samples are not guaranteed to be independent; they are not even guaranteed to be identically distributed, since each x i is distributed conditional on the progressively enlarging filtration F X u i -1 for i = 1 , . . . , M .
texts.text: Obviously if X is not Markovian then the preceding analysis fails. In this case samples are not guaranteed to be independent; they are not even guaranteed to be identically distributed, since each x i is distributed conditional on the progressively enlarging filtration F X u i -1 for i = 1 , . . . , M .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If one assumes strict non-Markovianity of the observed sample path x , then (by definition) one cannot collect i.i.d. samples from x , as no matter how the path is partitioned each section will be conditional on a different filtration. This forces a path-by-path approach. In particular it is clear that we need to evaluate the whole path in order to incorporate filtration information.
texts.text: If one assumes strict non-Markovianity of the observed sample path x , then (by definition) one cannot collect i.i.d. samples from x , as no matter how the path is partitioned each section will be conditional on a different filtration. This forces a path-by-path approach. In particular it is clear that we need to evaluate the whole path in order to incorporate filtration information.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A simple experiment is the following: consider two model/parameter pairs ( P , θ ) and ( Q , ϕ ) . Suppose we know that P is non-Markovian and Q is Markovian; however, their marginal distributions are similar enough that naive methods will not be able to conclude that they are generated by different distributions. Suppose we observe a path x : [0 , T ] → R over a discrete mesh ∆ . We know that the dynamics of x are governed either by P or Q , but we do not know over which intervals on ∆ that this is the case. We then do the following: At given points ∆ ′ = { 0 < u 1 < u 2 < · · · < u M = T } , for j = 1 , . . . , M we can study x j = x | [ u j -1 ,u j ] under the similarity score function Σ P j , Q j where P j = { x | [ u j -1 ,u j ] : x ∼ P } . The same notation holds for Q . This allows us to directly evaluate our observed, conditional path sample in a like-for-like manner against our beliefs P , Q . Negative sample scores imply conformance to the model P ; positive score imply conformance to the model Q .
texts.text: A simple experiment is the following: consider two model/parameter pairs ( P , θ ) and ( Q , ϕ ) . Suppose we know that P is non-Markovian and Q is Markovian; however, their marginal distributions are similar enough that naive methods will not be able to conclude that they are generated by different distributions. Suppose we observe a path x : [0 , T ] → R over a discrete mesh ∆ . We know that the dynamics of x are governed either by P or Q , but we do not know over which intervals on ∆ that this is the case. We then do the following: At given points ∆ ′ = { 0 < u 1 < u 2 < · · · < u M = T } , for j = 1 , . . . , M we can study x j = x | [ u j -1 ,u j ] under the similarity score function Σ P j , Q j where P j = { x | [ u j -1 ,u j ] : x ∼ P } . The same notation holds for Q . This allows us to directly evaluate our observed, conditional path sample in a like-for-like manner against our beliefs P , Q . Negative sample scores imply conformance to the model P ; positive score imply conformance to the model Q .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Remark 4.2 (Rank of signature mapping used in Σ ) . Ideally, we would like to use the rank2 signature S 2 to define the constituent scoring rules of Σ P , Q , so the filtration information over [ u j -1 , u j ] can be included when conducting inference. However, the methodology used to construct the conditional embeddings (from [SLL + 21]) cannot be used in the context of many-to-one comparisons. Thus, we must use the rank 1 signature in our experiments.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. The regime classification problem
texts.text: 5. The regime classification problem

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this section we give results for our approach to the market regime classification problem (MRCP) outlined in Section 3.3.
texts.text: In this section we give results for our approach to the market regime classification problem (MRCP) outlined in Section 3.3.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this section, we compare our clustering technique to one explored is a previous work [HIM21], in which a modification of the classical k -means algorithm was used to cluster regimes. For a price path s ∈ S ( R ) , regimes in this context were defined as empirical measures µ ∈ P p ( R ) extracted from the steam of log-returns associated to s . This motivated the use of the Wasserstein distance W p as the metric between elements to be clustered, and the Wasserstein barycenter was used to aggregate measures into centroids. This method (dubbed Wasserstein k -means ) was shown to perform strongly over other classical regime classification methodologies for streaming data over R .
texts.text: In this section, we compare our clustering technique to one explored is a previous work [HIM21], in which a modification of the classical k -means algorithm was used to cluster regimes. For a price path s ∈ S ( R ) , regimes in this context were defined as empirical measures µ ∈ P p ( R ) extracted from the steam of log-returns associated to s . This motivated the use of the Wasserstein distance W p as the metric between elements to be clustered, and the Wasserstein barycenter was used to aggregate measures into centroids. This method (dubbed Wasserstein k -means ) was shown to perform strongly over other classical regime classification methodologies for streaming data over R .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: However, this method was not without drawbacks. First, it is difficult to run the Wasserstein k -means algorithm for d > 1 in a manner which is computationally feasible. There do exist methodologies to make the calculation simpler by employing the (max) sliced Wasserstein distance and barycenter from [RPDB11, KNS + 19], however in practice the authors have found this approach unstable and computationally expensive. The second major drawback is that in aggregating the log-returns associated to s , one loses information about the order in which the returns appeared. Thus, on large enough timescales, changes in auto-correlative dynamics can be lost, which may be of keen interest to a practitioner. Given that returns in financial markets exhibit such properties, an algorithm which was able to capture these effects at little to no cost would be preferable.
texts.text: However, this method was not without drawbacks. First, it is difficult to run the Wasserstein k -means algorithm for d > 1 in a manner which is computationally feasible. There do exist methodologies to make the calculation simpler by employing the (max) sliced Wasserstein distance and barycenter from [RPDB11, KNS + 19], however in practice the authors have found this approach unstable and computationally expensive. The second major drawback is that in aggregating the log-returns associated to s , one loses information about the order in which the returns appeared. Thus, on large enough timescales, changes in auto-correlative dynamics can be lost, which may be of keen interest to a practitioner. Given that returns in financial markets exhibit such properties, an algorithm which was able to capture these effects at little to no cost would be preferable.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The method outlined in Section 3.3 is indeed able to make up for the shortcomings of the Wasserstein approach. We are able to cluster higher-dimensional paths at little computational cost, and by using the signature kernel k sig from eq. (17), the order in which path elements appear is captured.

We are able to cluster higher-dimensional paths at little computational cost, and by using the signature kernel k sig from eq. (17), the order in which path elements appear is captured.

To illustrate this, we constructed a regime-changed path ˆ s ∈ T ∆ ([0 , T ]; R d ) and set d = 10 . Here, we cannot easily apply the Wasserstein algorithm. One might expect that increasing the dimensionalty renders results less stable or less accurate. However, Figure 17 confirms that (in this setting) this is not the case.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 6. Real data experiments
texts.text: 6. Real data experiments

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this section we provide experiments on real market data using our anomaly detection technique. We seek to leverage the ease at which we can study higher-dimensional paths by tackling both the MRDP and MRCP on a basket of equities and cryptocurrency pairs respectively. We conclude the section with an example of how one can employ our approach as part of a wholly data-driven detection pipeline, operating on a path-by-path basis.
texts.text: In this section we provide experiments on real market data using our anomaly detection technique. We seek to leverage the ease at which we can study higher-dimensional paths by tackling both the MRDP and MRCP on a basket of equities and cryptocurrency pairs respectively. We conclude the section with an example of how one can employ our approach as part of a wholly data-driven detection pipeline, operating on a path-by-path basis.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 6.1. Basket of equities. We begin by studying the price path associated to a basket of equities s = ( s 1 , . . . , s d ) . Here we focused on US equities, and initially took the daily close prices from Yahoo Finance of Coca-Cola (NYSE:KO), IBM (NYSE:IBM), General Electric (NYSE:GE), Proctor and Gamble (NYSE:PG), Exxon Mobil (NYSE:XOM), JP Morgan (NYSE:JPM), and the S&P 500 (NYSE:ˆGSPC), giving a (time-augmented) price path ˆ s ∈ T ∆ ([ a, b ]; R 8 ) . Here, ∆ represents the mesh-grid associated to daily close prices. In this example we have a = 1980-01-01 and b = 2021-12-20.

Here, ∆ represents the mesh-grid associated to daily close prices. In this example we have a = 1980-01-01 and b = 2021-12-20.

texts.orig: We apply a wholly non-parametric approach by using the auto evaluator from Subsection 3.2.2 to calculate the score vector A L (ˆ s ) with L = { 4 , 8 , 12 } . We use the path transformer function Φ = ϕ time ◦ ϕ norm ◦ ϕ λ scale with λ = dt -1 / 2 (where dt = 1 / 252 as we are studying daily returns). We set h = (8 , 8) . The choice of detector is given by the rank 1 MMD associated to the RBF-lifted signature kernel with σ = 1 . Finally, we set α = 0 . 95 and allow for the empirical prior distribution to be comprised of the previous 200 MMDscores.

The dashed blue line is the true score vector A L (ˆ s ) for either detector, and the solid blue line is an exponential moving average. For validation purposes we give a plot of the same MMD score vector from Figure 18 against the VIX index, from January 2018 till December 2021. Up to a lag (due to the sampling frequency of path objects versus single return values) the auto-evaluator largely tracks the VIX well. Interestingly, periods of similarity in the VIX process do not necessary correlate to similar lagged MMD scores.

texts.orig: We conclude this section by clustering the price path ˆ s via our method described in Section 3.3. More concretely, we apply the hierarchical clustering used in Section 5 with maximum linkage, and initially set the number of clusters k = 2 . The results of running the clustering algorithm are presented in Figure 20. Here, the S&P 500 is plotted for reference, coloured according to the assigned cluster label. More translucent colouring means that a path segment had different labels assigned to it depending on what ensemble it was a part of. We plot the results of choosing k = 2 or k = 4 clusters.

The results of running the clustering algorithm are presented in Figure 20. Here, the S&P 500 is plotted for reference, coloured according to the assigned cluster label. More translucent colouring means that a path segment had different labels assigned to it depending on what ensemble it was a part of. We plot the results of choosing k = 2 or k = 4 clusters.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The results of either clustering algorithm are very similar to those obtained in Figure 18. The grey line represents the average cluster membership of the given sub-path segment s ∈ SP h (ˆ s ) . The main observation here is that the average cluster membership does not oscillate too wildly. The same is true even with an increase in the number of clusters chosen: Figure 20b gives results if we set k = 4 . Periods of market turmoil are also associated with periods of oscillating cluster membership. This is most evidenced by the periods leading up to and including the GFC, the dot-com bubble, and the recent market turbulence due to the pandemic.

texts.orig: 6.2. Cryptocurrency data. In this section, we run our auto MMD detector over a high-dimensional path of cryptocurrency pairs, all denominated in USDT (Tether). These include Bitcoin (BTC), Ethereum (ETH), Litecoin (LTC), Ripple (XRP), Binance Coin (BNB), and Polygon (MATIC). Our path we are interested in is given by ˆ s ∈ T ∆ ([ a, b ]; R 6 ) with ∆ the mesh grid associated to hourly close prices, a = 2019-06-01 and b = 2022-12-01. Due to increased frequency of the data, we study paths of size h = (8 , 32) , and apply the transformations ϕ norm ◦ ϕ time ◦ ϕ incr . We again use the auto-evaluator from Definition 3.10 equipped with the rank-1 RBF-lifted signature maximum mean discrepancy, and chose the smoothing parameter σ = 1 . We calculate A L (ˆ s ) with the lags L = { 8 , 16 } , representing the two most recent path ensembles that do not contain any sub-paths located in the currently evaluated sample. We set the memory of the auto-evaluator to the previous 250 MMD scores.

texts.orig: Figure 21 shows the rank 1 MMD score over EP h (ˆ s ) against the price of Bitcoin over the same period. We see three main regime change periods: in 2019, towards the end of 2020 and into 2021, and during mid-2022. Interestingly, the period during 2021 was not considered a regime change, which makes sense, as it closely resembles the period preceding it. Clustering results are given in Figure 22a. Here the price plot is the BTC/USDT hourly close prices, coloured according to the average cluster membership of each s ∈ SP h (ˆ s ) .

texts.orig: We wish to use the similarity score function Σ P , Q to evaluate path-by-path as opposed to taking ensembles. We calculate SP Φ h (ˆ s ) with h = (8 , 1) and take the standard time normalisation and increment transforms. To define our beliefs, we take here to be the clusters (cf. Sections 3.3 and 6.1) derived from the same price path over the date range [ a, b ] where a = 1980-01-01 and b = 2021-12-20. We take the case where k = 4 and chose our first set of beliefs to be all paths whose average cluster membership is less than (or equal to) 1 ; these represent more stable, persistent market conditions. Naturally the second set of beliefs represent periods of greater turmoil. We then calculate the similarity score vector for each sub-path extracted from ˆ s , where we use 64 paths sampled from each belief to calculate the unbiased estimators of the constituent scoring rules.

texts.orig: Figure 23 gives the value of Σ P , Q ( s ) for each sub-path extracted from SP Φ h (ˆ s ) . It is clear that, generally, we see similar results as per those in Section 6.1, with the added advantage that we do not need to take ensembles of paths, and we are comparing our results directly to reference measures as opposed to those extracted from the path itself. With our selection of hyperparameters, we see that the conformance score often does not breach above 0 (indicating periods of increased volatility or market turmoil). Towards the middle of last year, we see that the score was increasing. As of writing, the score is decreasing again, indicating a return to periods more similar to the standard regime. These observations are validated by the results seen in Figure 24.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 7. Conclusion
texts.text: 7. Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this work, we show that the (higher-rank) signature maximum mean discrepancy can be applied to a wide variety of online regime detection problems, and offline regime clustering problems. We showed that online regime detection could be performed in a parametric, beliefs-based setting and in a non-parametric setting. Common issues faced when using the MMD as a detection tool (ensemble evaluation, non-Markovianity of input data) were handled via the use of signature kernel scoring rules, and an associated similarity function. We showed that our methods worked well on both synthetic and real market data.
texts.text: In this work, we show that the (higher-rank) signature maximum mean discrepancy can be applied to a wide variety of online regime detection problems, and offline regime clustering problems. We showed that online regime detection could be performed in a parametric, beliefs-based setting and in a non-parametric setting. Common issues faced when using the MMD as a detection tool (ensemble evaluation, non-Markovianity of input data) were handled via the use of signature kernel scoring rules, and an associated similarity function. We showed that our methods worked well on both synthetic and real market data.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Topics for future research include pursing a deeper understanding of the two-sample test associated to D r sig . In particular it is of interest to determine how the terms of the signature contribute to the value of the MMD between two sets of paths P , Q . If P , Q are comprised of only a small number of path objects, sampling variance can cause a higher incidence of Type II error than would be expected. This has implications not only to regime detection, but to any work which utilizes the signature kernel MMD as a twosample testing tool (generative modelling, calibration, and so on). In this work we have seen that this issue can be circumvented by using an appropriate path transformation or path scaling ( σ bandwith parameter in the static RBF kernel). It is thus of interest to understand how each of these concepts are related to each other.
texts.text: Topics for future research include pursing a deeper understanding of the two-sample test associated to D r sig . In particular it is of interest to determine how the terms of the signature contribute to the value of the MMD between two sets of paths P , Q . If P , Q are comprised of only a small number of path objects, sampling variance can cause a higher incidence of Type II error than would be expected. This has implications not only to regime detection, but to any work which utilizes the signature kernel MMD as a twosample testing tool (generative modelling, calibration, and so on). In this work we have seen that this issue can be circumvented by using an appropriate path transformation or path scaling ( σ bandwith parameter in the static RBF kernel). It is thus of interest to understand how each of these concepts are related to each other.

texts.orig: Remark A.5 . The value of p ≥ 1 determines the integration theory required to define the elements of eq. (41). For instance, if p ∈ [1 , 2) , they can be thought of in the sense of Young integration. If X : [0 , T ] → E is of bounded variation, then the integrals can be thought of in the Riemann-Stieltjes sense. Larger values of p require rough path theory. As stated in the body, paths passed to the signature mapping are thought of as piecewise linear interpolants of discrete, time-augmented observations ˆ x = (( t 0 , x 0 ) , . . . , ( t N , x N )) . Thus they are always of bounded variation and the signature is always well-defined.

This is why path scalings are important tools when performing inference with signatures. The subject of optimal path scalings is a topic of future research.

If one is only comparing data which is expected to be regularly sampled (for example, daily price values) then one should ensure that this is reflected in the time component of each associated price path. As will be explained later, minor differences (due to data corruption or re-casting of variable types when loading or saving, for example) can result in erroneous conclusions regarding the similarity of paths.

texts.text: Again the relation that X ∼ 0 Y is equivalent to the stochastic processes X,Y having the same finite dimensional distribution. texts.orig: From Theorem 2.6, we know that for two stochastic processes X,Y if L ( X ) = L ( Y ) we have that E S ( X ) = E S ( Y ) . It follows that if X,Y are Markov, then they have the same adapted distribution. If they are not, then studying their finite-dimensional distributions is not enough to conclude that other, more sophisticated properties hold for the filtered processes X , Y ∈ FP I . A prime example is the following.
texts.text: From Theorem 2.6, we know that for two stochastic processes X,Y if L ( X ) = L ( Y ) we have that E S ( X ) = E S ( Y ) . It follows that if X,Y are Markov, then they have the same adapted distribution. If they are not, then studying their finite-dimensional distributions is not enough to conclude that other, more sophisticated properties hold for the filtered processes X , Y ∈ FP I . A prime example is the following.

texts.orig: Definition D.2 leads to the natural question: is m injective? If so, a candidate distance between measures on P ( X ) becomes apparent: the distance between their mean embeddings in H . Kernels with this property are called characteristic ([FGSS09], Section 2). Characteristicness of the Gaussian and Laplacian kernels on R d has been shown in [FGSS07]. Associated to characteristicness is the concept of universality : that for kernels on (compact) X , one has that the associated RKHS H is dense in C b ( X ) with respect to the L ∞ norm. As noted in [CO18], these two conditions are often equivalent, and we refer the reader to [SGS18] for more information. We can now conclude the following.

texts.orig: Then, one calculates the MMD between X π i and Y π i , which is stored. This builds an empirical distribution with n atoms and an associated critical threshold c α . One can then reject the null hypothesis if MMD b [ F , X, Y ] > c α . This method is also purely datadriven, but has computational complexity O ( n ( p + l )) where p is the cost of computing the permutations and l the cost of calculating the MMD. Thus for repeated evaluations of the MMD this approach is not ideal.

texts.text: Here we briefly outline the hierarchical clustering algorithm used for market regime classification problems in the paper. Recall that we are looking to cluster elements x ∈ S ( E ) where ( E,d ) is a metric space. The process of creating clusters via splitting (in the divisive case) or combining (agglomerative) is completed via a linkage criterion on elements of D . Denote by 2 V the power set of elements in E . Given two sets of elements A, B ∈ 2 E , common linkages ℓ include

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (1) Maximum linkage , ℓ ( A, B ) = max { d ( a, b ) : a ∈ A, b ∈ B } ,

texts.orig: Memory complexity for Algorithm 1 is Ω( n 2 ) (to store all pairwise elements) and runtime is O ( n 3 ) in the number of cluster elements. In our case, since the (rank 1) MMD between sets x = (ˆ x 1 , . . . , ˆ x n ) and y = (ˆ y 1 , . . . , ˆ y n ) is O ( n 2 l 2 d ) (where l is the length of the path, and d is the dimension) the overall runtime is O ( Nn 2 l 2 d ) where N is the number of cluster elements.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We note here that this approach is significantly more computationally expensive and memory intensive than ours outlined in Section 3.2. Computational complexity comes from calculating the matrix A from (59), which becomes a very expensive operation as the order of the truncated signature N increases: both since the matrix itself is R d N × d N and the inner product is taken over the truncated tensor algebra T 2 N ( R d ) which is functionally an operation in R d 2 N . Computational complexity also arises in the building of the null distribution D from (60): for each path p ∈ P 2 , in computing the infimum in (58) one necessarily has to compare the variance norm to each path in P 2 , an O ( n 2 ) operation where n = | P 2 | . Finally, the method does not scale well with dimension: terms in the signature grow exponentially in d as the order N increases. This means that storing and performing calculations with truncated signatures becomes infeasible. As we calculate the signature kernel from a PDE, our approach avoids this issue.
texts.text: We note here that this approach is significantly more computationally expensive and memory intensive than ours outlined in Section 3.2. Computational complexity comes from calculating the matrix A from (59), which becomes a very expensive operation as the order of the truncated signature N increases: both since the matrix itself is R d N × d N and the inner product is taken over the truncated tensor algebra T 2 N ( R d ) which is functionally an operation in R d 2 N . Computational complexity also arises in the building of the null distribution D from (60): for each path p ∈ P 2 , in computing the infimum in (58) one necessarily has to compare the variance norm to each path in P 2 , an O ( n 2 ) operation where n = | P 2 | . Finally, the method does not scale well with dimension: terms in the signature grow exponentially in d as the order N increases. This means that storing and performing calculations with truncated signatures becomes infeasible. As we calculate the signature kernel from a PDE, our approach avoids this issue.
